item.title: Character in Small Things: How a Child’s Habits Predict Their Future
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Proverbs 20:11 — “Even a child is known by his doings.”

There are many different ways we can come to know someone. Sometimes, we know a person just by seeing them. Almost every day, as I pass by the Pennsylvania Hospital at the corner of Eighth and Spruce Streets, I see a poor, blind Black man standing by the wall. He begs and sells matches. I can recognize him from half a block away. And if I saw him anywhere else, I’d know him immediately—I know him by sight. But I don’t know where he lives, whether he has a family, or what kind of person he is. I only know what he looks like.

Sometimes, we know a person by their name. Everyone in this country knows the name Franklin Pierce, the President of the United States, even though most people have never seen him. The same is true of Barnum, the famous showman—many people know his name, but wouldn’t recognize him if they saw him.

Other times, we know someone by description. Suppose you read a description of a man who committed a robbery: he has red hair, pale skin, crossed eyes, a big wart on his nose, is short, and limps on his left foot. Even if you’d never seen him before, you would recognize him from this description if you saw him.

But most importantly, people can be known by their actions—by what they do. This is how we all know Noah: he built the ark. We know Moses as the man who led the Israelites out of Egypt and through the wilderness. We know David as the one who killed the giant. Daniel is known as the man thrown into the lions’ den. Everyone knows Columbus as the man who discovered America, George Washington as the one who helped save his country, and Benedict Arnold as the traitor who betrayed it.

When we read history, we come to know people by their deeds—the things they did. This is the kind of knowing that Solomon means in our verse. It’s not about recognizing someone’s face, or knowing their name, or hearing a description of them. It’s about knowing them by their actions. And this is true for children just as it is for adults. The wise man says, “Even a child is known by his doings.”

Now, there are two questions we should think about with this verse.

First: What does “doings” mean here? Second: What can we “know” about a child from these doings?

When Solomon speaks of a child’s “doings,” I believe he means three things: —the tempers he shows, —the habits he builds, —and the friends he chooses.

Children Are Known by Their Tempers

The kind of temper a child has is part of what reveals their character. “Temper” means the feelings and attitudes we show toward others. Our temper plays a big role in shaping who we are.

Sometimes we look at people through something that changes how they seem—like colored glass. If I look at you through green glass, you look green. Through red glass, you look red. But you are not actually green or red—it's just the glass that makes it look that way. That’s a wrong way of judging.

But when we look at people through their temper—how they act toward others—we get a true picture of who they really are. Our tempers show our real selves.

Children have different tempers just as they have different hair or eye colors. Some children have harsh, angry tempers. If you speak to them, they respond sharply and rudely. They act like a bad-tempered dog that growls at everyone. If you ask them for even a small favor, they refuse with a rough, unkind answer.

But other children have kind tempers. When you speak to them, they always reply kindly. They’re eager to help, ready to do what they can to make others happy. They go through life like little sunbeams, bringing warmth and joy wherever they are.

Some children have fretful tempers. They’re always unhappy about something. They complain about the weather—it’s either too hot, too cold, too wet, or too dry. They complain about their clothes. Listen to one of these children getting dressed. He picks up his coat and grumbles, “What a coat! Who ever saw anything like it? It's about half a mile too big!” Of course, people who fret often exaggerate and don’t tell the truth. Then he picks up each item and complains—his stockings are too thin, his shoes too thick, one thing is too long, another too short, one too tight, another too loose.

These children complain about their food too. It’s either overcooked or undercooked, too plain or too fancy, or there’s always something wrong with it that they must mention.

Other children have patient tempers—the opposite of fretful ones. They don’t complain about the weather because they trust that God, who sends it, knows what’s best. They don’t complain about their clothes because they remember that many people have far worse. And they don’t complain about their food because they know that even if it’s not perfect, it’s better than having nothing at all.

There’s a story about two gardeners whose crops of peas were ruined by frost. One of them fretted and grumbled, saying no one else had such bad luck. Later, he visited his neighbor and was surprised to see a new crop of peas growing. “What are these?” he asked. “These are the ones I planted while you were fretting,” the neighbor replied. “Don’t you ever fret?” “Yes,” said the other, “but I wait until after I’ve fixed the problem.” “But then there’s no reason to fret at all.” “Exactly,” said the neighbor, “and that’s why I put it off.”

Some children have selfish tempers. They always think of themselves first and take the best for themselves. There was a little girl named Mary who showed this kind of temper. One day, she went out visiting with her mother, while her little brother Charlie stayed at home. The lady they visited gave Mary two peaches—one was plump, ripe, and juicy; the other was bruised, with a large rotten spot on the side.

Mary quickly began eating the good peach. After a moment, her mother asked, “Mary, aren’t you going to save some for Charlie?” “Oh yes, Ma,” Mary replied, “I’m saving the rotten one for Charlie!”

Children who give in to selfishness like this often grow up to be selfish adults. In the Bible, God once rebuked the people of Israel for offering Him only their blind and lame animals as sacrifices, while keeping the best for themselves. The same selfish spirit that made Mary “keep the rotten one for Charlie” is the spirit that caused the Israelites to offer God what they didn’t want.

We can still see this kind of selfishness today. Look at the plate after the church’s Communion offering. There’s a fake coin, and some others that are so worn and worthless they wouldn’t be accepted in a store. No one would trade meat or potatoes for them—but someone thought they were good enough to give to God.

Whenever I see one of those coins, I think, “That was given by someone who, as a child, was used to keeping the rotten one for Charlie.”

Some children have generous tempers. They enjoy sharing what they have with others. If they get a cake, a pie, or something special to eat, they don’t sneak off to eat it all by themselves. Instead, they gladly share it with their brothers, sisters, or friends. It makes them happier, and they enjoy their part more when it’s shared.

It is said of King Alfred, the great and good king of England, that when he was driven from his throne by the Danes and had to live in hiding and poverty, he once had only part of a loaf of bread for food. While he was in this state, a hungry beggar came to him and asked for help. Alfred opened his bag and freely shared the last of his bread with the poor man. A person who could be so generous as a man must have learned to be generous as a child. These generous tempers, when practiced, are part of the “doings” by which a child may be known.

But a child can also be known by the habits he forms, not just the tempers he shows. A habit is the way we are used to doing something. Someone once said, “Man is a bundle of habits.” This is just as true for boys and girls as it is for grown-ups. In fact, it is in childhood that we tie together the habits we will carry through life. And since we carry that bundle forever, we should be careful what we put into it.

Some children form lazy habits. They like to stay in bed late in the morning. It’s hard to wake them, and when they do get up, it’s hard to get them to study, work, or do anything useful—they just want to play or wander about. These children are like a farmer’s horse. The farmer said his horse had only two faults: he was hard to catch, and when caught, he wasn’t good for anything.

Other children form industrious habits. They get up early, study hard, and learn their lessons well. When they are given work, they do it cheerfully. They don’t get tired quickly but keep going until the job is finished. People like this almost always succeed in life. There is no difficulty that steady effort can’t overcome.

Once, a load of coal was dumped in front of a poor family’s cellar door. A little girl came out with a very small shovel and began to move it. A gentleman walking by said, “My child, you’ll never get all that coal in with that little shovel.” “Oh yes, I will, sir,” she replied, “if I only work LONG enough.”

There was once a poor boy who made up his mind to get an education. He had to work all day, and in the evening, he had no place to study and no light to read by. So he would take his book and go out into the street, standing by a shop window to study by the light shining from it. Sometimes, when the shops closed before he finished, he would climb up a lamppost, hold on with one hand, and hold his book with the other. It’s no surprise that he became a man known for his learning.

Some children develop careless habits. They never put things where they belong. Instead, they leave them wherever they happen to drop them. Then when they need those things, they don’t know where to look.

Imagine visiting a family where several children have such habits. Morning comes, and it’s time for school. You hear a loud commotion near the entryway. Curious, you go to your door and hear voices shouting: “Where’s my hat?” “Where’s my bonnet?” “Who took my books?” “Someone’s always taking my things! I wish people would leave my stuff alone!”

But the truth is—no one took their things. Everything is just where they left it: one item in the parlor, another in the dining room, another in the kitchen.

Careless habits may seem small, but they can lead to serious trouble. Years ago, a terrible gunpowder explosion happened in Wilmington, Delaware. Three large wagons were carrying gunpowder in kegs from Mr. Dupont’s mills on the Brandywine to a place near the Delaware River. As the wagons passed near Bishop Lee’s house, there was a sudden flash—a loud explosion—and everything was destroyed. The wagons, horses, drivers, and anything nearby were blown to pieces.

No one could say for sure what caused it. But if the truth were known, it may have been an act of carelessness. Imagine a cooper—someone who makes barrels—had careless habits. Suppose he picked up a stave with a small hole in it, didn’t notice it or didn’t care, and used it to make one of the powder kegs. The keg was filled with powder and loaded into the wagon. As the wagon moved, the powder shook out through the hole. Then a spark—maybe from a horse’s shoe or another source—hit the spilled powder, and the explosion followed.

Many terrible tragedies have come from just one careless act.

Other children develop careful habits. They don’t waste anything. Whether it’s time, money, or other things, they remember the words of Jesus: “Gather up the fragments, that nothing be lost.” They always put things where they belong and can quickly find them again. Their rule is: “A place for everything, and everything in its place.” It’s a wise rule, and following it brings great benefits. Children who build these habits often grow up to be both wealthy and useful.

Two gentlemen were once collecting donations for the Bible Society. As they passed a large, fine house, they overheard the man of the house scolding his servants for wasting the ends of candles and half-used lamp-lighters. One of the collectors said, “Let’s not bother stopping here. A man who is so strict about candle-ends won’t give much for the Bible.” “It won’t hurt to try,” said the other.

They went in and were surprised to receive a very generous donation. One of them said, “Sir, your gift amazes us. Just before we came in, we heard you telling your servants to save even the ends of candles. That made us think it wasn’t worth stopping.” The man replied, “Ah, gentlemen, it’s because I’ve learned to be careful with little things that I’m able to give generously to the Bible Society and other good causes.”

A young man once went to Paris, hoping to find a job. He carried letters of recommendation to a well-known banking firm. When he met the head of the firm, the man glanced over the letters and said, “We have nothing for you to do, sir.” The young man was crushed. He wanted to cry, but there was nothing he could do. So he bowed politely and walked away.

As he passed by the building, he noticed a tiny metal tack lying on the ground. He bent down, picked it up, and tucked it carefully under the collar of his coat. The banker, still watching from the window, saw this small act. He thought, “Any young man who’s attentive enough to pick up something so small at a moment like that would make a trustworthy employee.” He immediately sent someone to call him back.

The young man was offered a humble position. Over time, through steady faithfulness and good habits, he rose to become the head partner in the firm. Eventually, he became one of the wealthiest men in Paris—the city’s chief banker. His success began with a quiet act of diligence. You could say he made his fortune by a tack.

Some children develop dilatory habits. They’ll do what they’re told—but never right away.

Take Mary, for example. She’s a dilatory girl. If her mother says, “Mary, go upstairs and bring me the baby’s blue frock from the closet,” Mary replies, “Yes, Ma, I’ll go in a minute.” But instead of going, she keeps reading or playing, and her mother has to wait fifteen minutes.

John is the same way. One day, his father told him, “John, take this letter to the post office right after dinner.” “Yes, sir,” John replied. But after dinner, he went off to play for a while, and by the time he remembered the letter, it was too late for the mail. This caused his father a serious loss.

A boy like that will grow up always running behind. He’ll miss the boat by a few minutes when traveling. He’ll learn to delay small tasks as a child, and that habit will follow him into big decisions as an adult. This is how many people lose their souls—they put off the most important thing until it’s too late.

When Queen Elizabeth of England was dying, she cried out, “An inch of time! Millions of money for an inch of time!” She lay on a splendid bed. She had once worn a new dress every day and owned ten thousand more. She ruled over a vast kingdom where the sun never set. Yet none of that mattered in her final hour. She had lived seventy years but had delayed preparing for eternity until the very end. What should have taken a lifetime, she tried to squeeze into a few last moments. Then, too late, she would have given her whole kingdom for “an inch of time.”

Other children form prompt habits. When told to do something, they do it immediately. If they’re reading when called, they put the book down at once. If they’re playing, they stop and obey without delay. This habit is essential for success in life.

General George Washington was known for always being on time—never late for any engagement in his entire life. This is a powerful and valuable habit, and every person who wants to succeed should learn to live this way.

Children Are Known by Their Company

And then, the company a child keeps is another part of those “doings” by which he may be known. Choosing the right friends is very important. Few things shape our character more than the people we spend time with. You can often tell what kind of person someone is by noticing the kind of company they choose.

There is a little creature called the chameleon. It changes color to match the light or the surface it is on. In a way, people are like that too. We tend to become like those we spend time with. That’s why it matters so much who our companions are.

There was once a father who was very careful about the company his children kept. One day, he told his daughter Susan and her brother not to join a certain group of people, even though they were eager to do so.

“Dear father,” Susan said, “you must think we’re very childish if you believe we couldn’t be around them without being harmed.”

Without saying anything, the father took a piece of dead coal from the fireplace and handed it to Susan.

“It won’t burn you, my child,” he said. “Take it.”

She did, and at once her white hand was stained black. Her dress was smudged too.

“We must be very careful when we handle coals,” she said, annoyed.

“Yes,” her father replied. “Because even when coals don’t burn, they still blacken. And that’s exactly how it is with bad company.”

So then, these are the things by which a child may be known—his tempers, his habits, and the company he keeps. And that gives us the answer to our first question.

What May Be Known of a Child by his Doings

Now, the other question is: What may be known of a child by his doings? This can be answered more briefly than the first. The answer is this: you can tell a child’s fortune by his doings.

Some wicked people claim to be fortune-tellers. They pretend to know what will happen to a person in the future. And many are foolish enough to believe them. These people think of “fortune” as something that happens by chance. But there is no such thing as fortune in that sense.

The word “fortune” comes from the name of a Roman idol called Fortuna. She was pictured as a woman, blindfolded, holding a horn of plenty, from which she poured out blessings at random, without knowing who received them. But that is a heathen idea. There is no such person or power.

We know from the Bible that our blessings do not come by luck or chance. Scripture says, “Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and comes down from the Father of lights.” And again, it says that God gives us “life, and breath, and all things.” But unlike the idol Fortuna, God does not give blindly. He gives with full knowledge—He sees clearly what He gives, and to whom. And He gives His best gifts to those who love Him.

Now, when we say you can tell a child’s fortune by his doings, we do not mean whether he will be rich or powerful, for those are not God's best blessings. God's best gifts are His grace, His Spirit, and the blessings of salvation. These He gives to those who obey and love Him.

So what does this have to do with a child’s doings? Everything. God has commanded us to live in a certain way. If we obey, He promises to bless us. Without His blessing, there is no true fortune. So if you want to know whether a person is likely to receive God’s blessing, you must look at what he does—at his doings.

And what are those doings? They are the tempers he indulges, the habits he forms, and the company he keeps.

Now, show me a child who is cross, fretful, and selfish in temper; who is lazy, careless, and always putting things off; and who chooses friends who are just like him—or even worse—and I can tell you that child’s future as surely as I can tell you that two times five equals ten. That child will grow up to be poor, unhappy, and useless in this life; and, unless he repents, he will be miserable forever in the life to come.

But show me a child who is trying, with God’s help, to be kind, patient, and generous in temper; who is diligent, careful, and prompt in habit; who keeps company with those who love and fear God, and desires to be like them—and I can tell you that child’s future just as easily.

You can tell what a farmer’s future will be when you see him rising early, working late, plowing, planting, and caring for his land with steady effort and attention. You can tell what a merchant’s future will be when you see him faithful at his post, doing everything he can to make his business succeed.

Solomon says, “Do you see a man diligent in his business? He shall stand before kings.” In other words—he will surely succeed.

Bear in mind, my dear children, that you are making your fortunes every day. You’ve read stories about people “going off to seek their fortunes,” but you can do that just as well—and even better—by staying at home. Right now, through the tempers you show, the habits you’re forming, and the company you’re keeping, you are shaping your future.

What kind of tempers, habits, and company are yours? That’s a very important question. You should carefully examine yourself to see what might be wrong, and ask God to help you correct it immediately. It’s easy to do now—but it will be much harder later.

The Fable of the Crocodile and the Ichneumon

Remember the fable of the crocodile and the ichneumon. A huge, fierce crocodile once lived along the banks of the Nile River. He brought fear and destruction wherever he went, devouring both shepherds and sheep, herdsmen and cattle. Everyone fled from him. Many plans were made to destroy him, but all failed.

At last, the people held a public meeting to figure out what to do. As they discussed, a small creature called the ichneumon stepped forward. (The ichneumon is a lizard-like animal that eats crocodile eggs.)

He said: “I see your distress, friends. I can’t help with this great crocodile now, but I can give you some advice for the future. A little wisdom is worth more than all your bravery. It might be glorious to defeat a great evil—but it’s far wiser to stop it before it grows. You ignore the crocodile when he is small and weak. But once he grows strong, you fear him and flee. I may be just a tiny creature, but I’m more useful to the country than any of you. I stop the crocodile while he’s still in the egg. While you struggle for months with just one grown crocodile, I destroy fifty in a single day.”

MORAL: This fable, dear child, is intended to show The danger of suffering ill habits to grow; For the fault of a week may be conquered, ’tis clear, Much easier than if it went on for a year.

Conclusion

Now, let me urge you to follow the example of the ichneumon. When you notice a bad temper or habit beginning to grow in your heart, deal with it at once—attack it while it is still in the egg. Don’t wait until it becomes like the crocodile: strong, fierce, and beyond your control.

But don’t try to fight these things in your own strength. If you want to succeed, you must ask Jesus to help you. “Without Him, we can do nothing.” But with His help, we can do all things.

If God gives us His grace, then our ways will be ordered to please Him. We will begin to love what He commands, and long for what He promises. And in the middle of all the many changes and troubles in the world, our hearts will be fixed where true joy is found.

May the Lord make this true for each one of us—for Jesus’ sake. Amen.

item.title: A Father's Fury Against His Daughter's Calling
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: Mrs. Dorothy Jones, wife of Thomas Jones, missionary to the West Indies. This chapter is from Woman's Work in the Mission Field; or, The Special Ministry of Christian Women to the Heathen (not yet a finished audiobook on our site). When Dorothy was about fifteen, her mother died. Mrs. Hobson had been a sincere Christian, a wise parent, and a woman of insight. Sensing that there might be disagreements between Dorothy and her father about religion, she made a final request before her death: that if Dorothy—or any of the other children—ever chose to attend a different church than the one in which they were raised, their father would allow it. This loss had a deep spiritual effect on Dorothy. It eventually led her to make what she called “the great decision.” For a time, she was deeply troubled by her sense of sin. Her health declined, and she had to leave home for a while. During this time, a friend invited her to the Wesleyan Chapel in Burslem. She kept attending the Wesleyan services, and after much inner struggle and difficulty, she came to believe in Christ with all her heart. She found peace and assurance of forgiveness through His grace. Soon after, she joined the Wesleyan Church. In the early joy of her faith, she began teaching in Sunday school, collecting for missions, and taking part in other Christian work. During the week, she supported herself by painting designs on china at a nearby pottery. This job became a great help to her when a serious trial came. Her father became angry about her devotion to religion and her involvement with the Wesleyans. He warned her that unless she gave up these things, she would have to leave his house. She reminded him of her mother’s dying request, but he would not change his mind. One night, after she returned from collecting for the missionary society, he said, “Dorothy, I’m giving you half an hour to decide. Either you stop praying, visiting the sick, collecting for the blacks, and going to those missionary meetings—or you leave this house.” Dorothy, though afraid and sorrowful, stood firm in her faith. She replied, “Father, I cannot promise that. I must serve Jesus first.” “Then you must go,” he said. Dorothy later wrote, “It was a great trial to me, but my heart was kept trusting in the Lord.” At the end of the half-hour, she left home. The servant girl went with her to carry her things. With no relatives nearby, she went to the home of a poor widow, who happened to be at home that night because of a sprained wrist, which had kept her from locking the door. Dorothy stayed there for a time, continuing her work and praying that God would change her father’s heart. God answered her prayer. Mr. Hobson soon sent her a warm invitation to return home, promising never again to interfere with her beliefs. She returned gladly and gratefully, and with even greater zeal to serve Christ. Not long after, Miss Hobson became engaged to Rev. Thomas Jones, who was preparing for missionary service. This brought more trouble at home. Despite all his earlier harshness, Mr. Hobson considered Dorothy his favorite child. But the thought of losing her made him furious. He began intercepting Mr. Jones’s letters and even threatened to harm him if he came near the house. One evening, the situation came to a head. Mr. Hobson entered the room where Dorothy was sitting, seemingly ready to talk things through. Dorothy silently prayed, then calmly told her father that Mr. Jones had passed his examination, had been appointed to the West Indies, and wanted to know if she could go with him. “I suppose,” said Mr. Hobson, “if I say no, you’ll still go?” “No, father,” she answered. “I wouldn’t think it right to go if you forbade it, and the mission committee wouldn’t allow it either—their rules prevent it.” “You don’t know what you’re doing,” he said. “You won’t be happy with all those blacks around you. And the climate—it’s so unhealthy, you won’t live long there.” “I’ve thought about all those things, father,” she replied. “I’ve read everything I could on the subject.” “Then what more do you want? Can’t you just be happy at home?” She answered with deep emotion, “If I could only be useful to the heathen, I would be happy.” He paused, then said, “Well, I neither say yes nor no. Please yourself.” “Then, father,” she said, “I will take it as my duty to go, since you do not forbid it.” And so the matter was settled. The young missionaries were married in Burslem on October 22nd, 1824, and sailed for the mission field on December 21st. After various delays, they arrived safely in Antigua on February 7th, 1825, and landed at English Harbour.

item.title: Trading Comfort for Dependence: How God Became My Provider
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
From: John and Betty Stam

At the time, John noticed what seemed to be a slight note of worry in his father’s letters about the finances of the Star of Hope Mission. Since the mission was supported entirely by voluntary gifts—with no offerings ever taken—any strain was felt deeply. Though John hesitated to speak of his own experience, he finally wrote home with humility and quiet joy in the spring of 1931, after his first visit to Elida:

I would not mention what I am going to tell you now, if the Mission were not in the position it is. But I do want to give praise to God for the way He has led me during the past year. About twelve months ago, when I began to come to the end of the money I had brought with me to the Institute, I told the Lord that if I were to go to China, I must know Him as the answerer of prayer here in the homeland.

And the Lord has wonderfully shown Himself to me as Jehovah-jireh, and that right here in Chicago. Someday, perhaps just before I leave for China, I may tell you some of these experiences. If I did not know before that God works today just as He did of old, I know it now. And may I mention some of the lessons I have had to learn?

First, that it is all of grace. God does not supply our needs as a reward for faithfulness—we are unprofitable servants at best.

Second, that it is useless to pray unless we have searched the Word, and allowed it to search us (Psalm 139:23–24), even our thoughts toward others, our motives and desires. I once had to wait three days for urgently needed help to learn this lesson.

Third, that we are not to depend on our faith, but on God’s faithfulness. Our faith is simply the hand held out to receive from His faithfulness.

Fourth, that if the answer seems delayed, there may be something in me causing the delay. In His faithfulness, God may withhold an answer rather than encourage a wrong attitude.

Fifth, that faith must rest intelligently on God’s revealed will. Not just on a strong personal desire, but on what His Word says is His will—then I can pray with confidence.

Sixth, that I must not expect God to answer in the way I suggest. The means, manner, and everything else must be left entirely to Him. We often keep looking to expected sources, forgetting that the real Source is the Lord, who can use anyone, anywhere, with equal ease.

How I thank Him for this past year! I would not trade it for all the ease a bank balance could bring. How could I have learned to trust the Lord, even a little, if everything had gone smoothly? How could He have tested me, had I not been completely dependent on Him? Of course He knows what we need! We can have blessed peace and rest, even when we have nothing to rely on but His promises...

The Bible has become a new book to me this past year.

Oh, how precious is that promise in Matthew 6:33! “Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and His righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.”

That’s a contract—between God and us. How poor would be our experience if we only relied on visible supplies, or the people who usually sent money! But the Living God—can He forget His own work? It is not our work, but His. His interest in it far surpasses ours.

As long as we live within His will, He cannot forget us. Could a mother forget her boys? No matter how hard you or Mother might try, you could not forget us...

Dear Dad, what a blessing it is that God thinks we are worth testing! A workman only spends time and effort on materials that can be made into something good. God will perfect all that concerns us—Hallelujah!

item.title: How Prayer Healed My Child Against All Odds
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
From Henry Frost books: Miraculous Healing.

To confirm what I’ve already said—that when God’s time comes, He can and often does heal in miraculous ways—I want to share a personal experience from our family life.

In 1908, our family included Mrs. Frost, our seven children, and myself. We lived in Germantown, Philadelphia, at 235 School Lane, the home of the China Inland Mission. God had greatly blessed us. All our children were believers, and we were all in excellent health. Our hearts were full of thankfulness to God, both individually and as a family, because we were experiencing His daily blessings in every area of life.

In early August that year, our family was somewhat scattered. The two older boys, our three daughters, and I were in Attica, in western New York. Our second-youngest son was visiting friends closer to home. That left Mrs. Frost and our youngest child alone in Germantown. I had gone away to rest and recover. The demanding work of the Mission and the intense summer heat in Germantown had left me physically drained, and I needed time to regain my strength.

Even though we were apart and I was unwell, everything in our family was going along smoothly and happily. This continued until the middle of the month. Then, on August 18, a serious accident happened. Our youngest child, F., a five-year-old boy, was badly injured. He had been playing in the dining room of the Germantown Home, went through a swinging door into the butler’s pantry, and climbed several shelves in the china pantry to reach a cup on the top shelf. As he stretched for it, he lost his balance, fell backward, and dropped about seven feet. He hit the back and right side of his head on the hard wooden floor below.

At that moment, the maid was setting the table in the dining room for lunch. She heard the fall, rushed into the pantry, and found the boy unconscious. She picked him up, carried him upstairs, and placed him in his mother’s arms. He had regained consciousness while being carried up. Mrs. Frost laid him on a bed in a back bedroom and called Dr. R., our Christian physician. But he was out of town, as were the other doctors she tried to contact. Dr. R. returned after four days, found F. in stable condition—though he had been seriously ill—advised rest and quiet, and said he believed everything would turn out well.

Mrs. Frost sent me this report while I was in Attica. Naturally, it was deeply alarming, and my first thought was to return to Germantown right away. But Dr. Ellinwood—Mrs. Frost’s father and my attending physician—strongly advised against it. He said my health couldn’t handle the heat of Germantown in my current condition. Also, Mrs. Frost soon assured me that I didn’t need to worry about F., so I stayed in Attica, and she continued caring for him.

Later, I learned that she had chosen not to tell me how serious some of his symptoms had become, because she didn’t want to bring me home. Because of this, I remained away for two more weeks. But as F.’s condition worsened, Dr. R. insisted she send a telegram for me. I left immediately and arrived in Germantown on September 5.

When I got there, I found that our child could eat very little, was having chills, running high fevers, and needed to stay mostly in a darkened room. He was suffering from intense head pain, often lasting up to ten hours at a time with only short breaks. It was clear that Dr. R. was not only deeply concerned but also confused. He believed F. was suffering from meningitis, but there were other complications he couldn’t explain.

When things reached this critical point, Dr. R. told us he had decided to call in two of Philadelphia’s leading physicians—Dr. B. and Dr. S.—for consultation. Dr. S. came once, and Dr. B. came twice. Together with Dr. R., they spent hours examining F., trying to determine exactly what illness was affecting him.

After the second examination by Dr. R. and Dr. B., they asked to speak with me in private. Then Dr. B. said:

“Mr. Frost, Dr. R. and I agree that F. is suffering from meningitis. We also believe it’s likely that an abscess has formed on his brain, under the spot where he hit his head. Because of this, we are recommending an operation—specifically, to trephine the skull. It is now up to you to decide whether or not this procedure should be done.”

I felt that before making any decision, I needed more information. So I asked:

“Dr. B., what chance is there that F. will recover without the operation?”

Dr. B. answered, “None at all.”

“What?” I said in shock, “Is the boy dying?”

“Well,” he replied, “he may live a few more days, but he will not recover.”

“Then,” I asked, “what chance is there if the operation is performed?”

He paused for a moment, then said, “I can’t be certain, but I would estimate about one in ten.”

That answer hit me hard. No hope without surgery, and only a one in ten chance with it. What should I do? I thought in silence and prayed. Finally, I said:

“Thank you, Dr. B., for your honesty. I’ll take the one chance in ten.”

This conversation with the doctors took place on the morning of Saturday, September 19. That evening, we brought F. in a comfortable carriage to Germantown Hospital. I left him there, while Mrs. Frost stayed with him. It was agreed that Dr. S. would perform the operation the next day at noon, with Dr. R. and Dr. B. also in attendance.

Our two older boys had come back to Germantown, and they went with me the next morning to the hospital. Both of them were a great comfort to us—especially our second son, who was studying medicine at the time. He was also a comfort to little F. When it was time for the operation, he carried F. from the private room, down the long corridor, into the operating room, and left him there with the three doctors and the nurses.

The operation started at noon and ended at 12:50. Afterward, Dr. R. and Dr. B. came out to the veranda where Mrs. Frost, our two sons, and I were waiting. They had come to report the outcome. Dr. R. spoke first. He said:

“Mr. Frost, the operation is finished, and it went well. But we didn’t find an abscess, and so we’re right back where we started.”

In the stress of the moment, I responded without thinking, in a way that may have seemed abrupt:

“Thank God! Now we know exactly where we are—it’s God or no one!”

“Yes,” said kind Dr. R., “you’re absolutely right—it’s God or no one.”

F. was taken back to his room. Later, we saw him lying there—his eyes closed, his face pale and ashy, and his head wrapped in a large bandage, leaving only the oval of his face visible. It was a heartbreaking and seemingly hopeless sight.

After this, a long battle for life began. F. couldn’t eat solid food, or if he did, he couldn’t keep it down. Because of this, the little strength and weight he had left were quickly fading. The responsibility of caring for him fell mostly on Mrs. Frost, because if she left his side, he would cry out for her with deep distress.

One day, his mother gently asked him,

“F., is there anything you’d like to eat?”

In a faint voice, he replied, “Corn flakes.”

I turned to the house doctor who was nearby and asked, “Shall we try them?”

The doctor didn’t answer, but only shrugged his shoulders, as if to say, “You can try, but it won’t help.” We gave him the corn flakes anyway, and to our surprise, he enjoyed them and kept them down. This small success brought a bit of improvement and gave us a faint hope.

On October 3, the hospital ambulance was able to take us and our dear boy back home to School Lane. Throughout F.’s long illness, we had been deeply moved by the kindness and concern shown to us—by friends, strangers, and the whole community, who had been touched by his suffering. Many Philadelphia physicians, partly out of professional interest, called to ask how he was doing.

We were especially moved to hear that, on the Sunday morning of his operation, F. had been publicly prayed for in six Germantown churches.

And so, our son was laid in his bed at home, and we resumed our watch at his side, full of care and longing.

The days that followed were filled with deep suffering. Before, F. had experienced chills, fever, and headaches every day. Now, these attacks came every other day—but they were far worse. His temperature would rise to 103 degrees, and once it reached 105. [The doctors confirmed it was not a case of malaria.] This meant the illness had grown more severe. When the attacks came, F. would quickly fall into agonizing pain, crying out again and again, “Oh mother, my head hurts, my head hurts!”

During these times, all Dr. R. could do was give opiates. All Mrs. Frost could do was place cold cloths on his aching head and hold his burning hand. All I could do was take my turn watching or step aside and pray. Again and again—hundreds, even thousands of times—now that the doctors had failed and despair was setting in, we begged God to come and heal. But no answer came. The boy only grew worse. It seemed certain he would die.

One Sunday around the middle of November, F. seemed slightly better. It was a day when the fever wasn’t expected. So Mrs. Frost dressed him and brought him into the sitting room for a change of scene. Since neither of us had been to church in weeks, we decided to attend the morning service at Westside Church, just a block and a half away. We left F. in the care of his oldest brother, whose gentle presence always comforted him.

We had felt hopeful as we left. But as we came walking back down School Lane, we heard the cries we had come to dread. We rushed into the house and up the stairs. There we found F. undressed and back in bed, in terrible pain. Mrs. Frost immediately took her place at his side, and for eighteen long, painful hours we watched over his suffering body.

At last, around six in the morning, F. collapsed into a deep sleep from sheer exhaustion. We lay down on our bed too and fell asleep. We slept until about nine o’clock. When we rose and got dressed, our son soon woke as well and asked to get up. Mrs. Frost dressed him and brought him once again into the sitting room.

It had now been four months since F. was injured. As many as twelve physicians had seen him, including specialists, and all of them admitted they could not understand his condition or offer a cure. This had led to much prayer—not only from us but also from many of our friends, who were earnestly asking God to heal our son. Yet even among them, uncertainty remained. Mrs. Frost and I continued to pray for God’s direct intervention, but we received no assurance that healing would come.

Looking back on those days, I can honestly say we never doubted God’s love, compassion, or His power to heal miraculously. But physically and mentally, we were completely worn out. Even Mrs. Frost, with all her strength and courage, was deeply exhausted. She had kept watch through countless nights, and spent many long, weary days at F.’s side, gently stroking his head or holding his hand—always in a darkened room, where both shutters and shades were drawn. Even the faintest light caused him sharp pain, like red-hot needles piercing his eyes and brain.

And now, after all these months, there was still no improvement. It seemed the doctors had given up—leaving us and our son in complete helplessness, teetering on the edge of hopelessness.

Then something extraordinary happened.

That morning, Mrs. Frost had hoped for a brief break. F.’s fever had spiked the day before, so—based on the usual pattern—she expected it wouldn’t return that day. But at eleven o’clock, she noticed the telltale signs: his face pale and tense, the early chill setting in, the fever beginning once more. It was almost more than she could bear.

She stood there, looking at her son in a daze. Then, suddenly, she was overcome with a holy impulse. Taking F. by the hand, she said, “Come with me.” She led him to her darkened bedroom, closed the door, knelt beside the bed with her beloved child, and cried out,

“O dear Father in heaven, I can never go through this again! Please don’t let him ever have another headache!”

And he never did. From that moment on, the sovereign and compassionate Lord—having brought us to the end of every human resource—chose to heal. Instantly, the chills, fever, and headaches were gone. From then forward, F. was able to eat and keep his food normally. He began gaining weight and strength. Each day he was dressed, and before long he was playing again—both inside and outside—just like any healthy child.

Since that day, there has never been the slightest sign of his former illness. In 1922, when he was nineteen years old, he was a freshman at Princeton University. During that time, the University’s Physical Director examined him and placed him in class A—meaning he was in excellent health. In the years that followed, he was on the tennis squad and one of the pitchers on the varsity baseball team.

He is now in business—sharp in mind, strong in body, and full of energy. His healing was exactly like the healings we read of in the New Testament: immediate, complete, and lasting. It was, without question, a miracle.

When I next saw Dr. R., I told him what had happened and said,

“Well, doctor, I suppose we must say that God did it.”

“Yes,” replied the Christian doctor, “there is no doubt about it—God did it!”

And everyone in the Germantown community agreed with us. Both believers and unbelievers alike were compelled to admit that a great and undeniable miracle had taken place among them.

Our son’s healing shows that God’s choices are the wisest, His timing the best, His ways perfect, and His love and compassion without limit. It also proclaims that Jesus Christ is truly the living Son of God, and that He still has power on earth—not only to forgive sins, but also to heal the sick.

So I give this, as my closing testimony in this book: it is my firm conviction that God is willing to answer our prayers for physical healing. And I also believe that if He delays or gives no answer at all, it is never because He does not love, care, or desire to heal, but because He has something better for us—something that time or eternity will reveal.

For this reason, I am more and more persuaded that, whether in health or in sickness, in life or in death, we can trust our heavenly Father with full and absolute confidence, holding fast to the truth:

“They who trust Him wholly

Find Him wholly true.”

Now unto Him that is able to do far more abundantly beyond all that we ask or imagine, according to the power working within us, to Him be glory in the church through Christ Jesus through all generations, forever and ever. Amen!

item.title: Jean Brugiere: The Martyr Who Smiled at His Executioner
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
The following was taken from chapter 9 of Cross and Crown.

During the reign of Francis the first, known as the "chivalrous king of France," many cruelties were inflicted on those who embraced the Reformed faith. As France began to grow in intellectual strength—much of it driven by the spread of the Scriptures—the clergy grew increasingly jealous. Anything that might weaken their control over the people was hated by them, and they worked hard to stop the spread of knowledge from the very beginning.

Those who accepted the Reformed faith believed education was a Christian duty. They saw ignorance as the root of all vice and resisted it completely. To the priests, then, learning and Protestantism became nearly the same thing. Beza writes that during this time, all learning was viewed with suspicion, and even some faithful Roman Catholics were accused of heresy simply for being more educated than others. But learning wasn’t the only offense. The priests often seemed to enjoy inventing reasons to arrest and kill people.

People could be condemned for failing to kneel when the Ave Maria bell rang, for not taking off their hats when passing statues of saints (which stood on nearly every street corner), or for eating meat on fast days. Clement Marot, the psalm-writer, was caught eating bacon during Lent and was imprisoned, barely escaping being burned at the stake. Heartbreaking scenes of suffering were common in Paris and other cities. Victims were regularly led to the stake, and when Francis finally died—worn down by disease caused by his immoral life—it brought a brief relief from the persecution.

One of the last martyrs under Francis’s rule was Jean Brugiere. Accused of heresy, he was imprisoned but refused to give up his faith. Wanting to preserve his life, he attempted to escape and succeeded. He lived in hiding for a time, but was eventually discovered and arrested again. Even then, he escaped once more. This happened several times until the authorities finally caught him, bound him in chains, and took him to Paris where the prisons were more secure.

In Paris, he was put on trial and found guilty of rejecting the teachings of the Roman Catholic Church. He was sentenced to be burned alive. The court ordered that the execution take place in Issoire, a town in southern France. He was taken from Paris to Montferrand and held there until the time set for his death. While imprisoned, the Chief Inquisitor, Ory, tried to convert him by debating the doctrine of the real presence in the Eucharist. Brugiere stood firm. Neither arguments nor threats could shake his faith.

“If you deny,” said Ory, growing desperate, “that the body of our Lord is in the Host when the priest speaks the sacramental words, then you deny God’s power, who can do all things.”

“I do not deny God’s power,” Brugiere replied. “But we are not debating whether God can do this—we are asking what He has done in His Holy Sacrament, and what He desires from us.”

Seeing that he could not be persuaded, the priests moved quickly to carry out the sentence. The distance from Montferrand—now part of the city of Clermont-Ferrand—to Issoire was twenty-six miles. On March 2, 1547, Brugiere was taken from his prison and handed over to a group of guards who would lead him to the stake. Several priests traveled with him. They hurried along the road and soon reached Issoire, lying in a deep valley along the River Creuse. There, the martyr spent his final night.

On March 3, he was led out to die. The priests who had come from Montferrand went with him to the place of execution. Along the way, they pleaded with him to recant and save his life, but he answered calmly and firmly that he was not afraid to die and would not risk his soul by denying the truth.

The stake had been set up in the town square. A large crowd had gathered, watching silently, deeply moved. One priest stepped forward with a crucifix, urging Brugiere to pray to the Virgin and the saints. But he gently turned the priest away and said with a peaceful smile:

“Let me think of God before I die. I am content with the only Advocate He has appointed for sinners.”

Then he turned to the executioner and, in a cheerful voice, told him to do his duty. The executioner, clearly unsettled by Brugiere’s calmness, fumbled awkwardly with the chains. Suddenly he slipped and fell at the martyr’s feet. Brugiere smiled, offered his hand, and said kindly:

“Take heart, M. Pouchet. I hope you’re not hurt.”

The executioner soon recovered and finished securing Brugiere to the stake. Then he lit the fire.

As the flames rose, Brugiere lifted his eyes to heaven and prayed in a clear, peaceful voice that could be heard throughout the square:

“O Heavenly Father, for the sake of Thy Son, please comfort me in this hour by Thy Holy Spirit, so that the work begun in me may be brought to completion—for Thy glory and for the good of Thy poor Church.”

The smoke and flames soon ended his voice. Yet he never screamed, never groaned. Quietly, steadily, the fire consumed him, until at last his spirit left the scorched body and entered the presence of God—clothed in the white robes of the redeemed, washed in the blood of the Lamb.

When it was over, the crowd, which had watched in reverent silence, went away quietly, deeply moved. They pitied the martyr, admired his strength, and many began to wonder if the man who died so peacefully had truly been right in his faith.

The parish priest of Issoire, who had taken no part in the persecution but had watched the execution, was overwhelmed by what he had seen. As he walked home, he said with deep feeling:

“May God give me grace to die in the faith of Brugiere.”

item.title: No Neutral Life: Living for Good or for Harm
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: Verse 27: He who diligently seeks good finds favor, but evil will come to him who searches for it. There is no such thing as a neutral life. Every one of us is living either for good or for harm. True happiness and real usefulness come from focusing our lives on one worthy aim—seeking what is good. This cannot be done casually. We must not wait for opportunities to come to us; we must look for them, seek them earnestly, rise early, and take joy in the work. We must remember that we carry the means of blessing others in our own hands. Every talent we have has a place to be used and can be “put out to interest” with rich returns. This may be in acts of “pure and undefiled religion”—helping the fatherless and the afflicted, teaching the ignorant, or training the young—the future of the church. Each of us should find what we can do, and whether it be little or much, do it prayerfully, faithfully, and wholeheartedly—not discouraged by small hindrances, and never using the excuse that doing little means doing nothing. When we live for others, we live for our truest happiness. In diligently seeking their good, we gain favor—often from people, always from God. He honors even a little strength spent for Him and accepts the single talent offered in His service. As Brainerd wisely said, “Filling every hour with some profitable labor, whether of heart, head, or hands, is an excellent means of spiritual peace and boldness before God.” But Satan gives his servants plenty of work—seeking harm. Their restless energy often puts our indifference to shame. Yet their evil often turns back on themselves, as it did for Satan. The harm he brought to mankind came upon his own head. His followers also often fall victim to their own deceit, and their ruin is worse for having dragged many others down with them. What, then, will be the result of my diligence—a blessing or a curse to those around me? O my God, let it be from You, and for You! (From Charles Bridges' commentary on the Proverbs.)

item.title: Prayer in Faith Better Than Faith in Prayer
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: There is a great difference between prayer in faith and faith in prayer. Faith in prayer is widespread—many people, religious or not, believe in the general idea of prayer “working.” But true prayer in faith is rare—so rare that Jesus Himself asked whether He would even find it on the earth when He returns. Prayer in faith is a duty God commands; faith in prayer is not commanded, nor is it justified. Prayer in faith is humble, spiritual, and rooted in truth. Faith in prayer, on the other hand, is often superstitious, presumptive, or simply misplaced—even when it springs from sincere but misled Christians. To pray in faith is to bring our requests before God with the confidence that if we ask according to His will, He hears us, and will answer in line with our faith. But faith in prayer assumes that merely saying the prayer will automatically produce results—that prayer itself, as a process, guarantees outcomes. Prayer in faith looks to the character and promises of the God we pray to. It is rooted in knowing Him and trusting Him. Faith in prayer rests on the act of praying itself—as if the prayer were a kind of power, regardless of who it’s directed to or whether it aligns with God’s will. Prayer in faith is the simple, trusting cry of a child to a loving Father. Faith in prayer is more like the vain repetitions of the pagans, who think they will be heard because they keep saying the right words over and over. A clear example of the difference came during a national crisis in 1881, when President James A. Garfield was shot and lay dying. Christians across the United States united in prayer for his recovery. While he lived, it was hard to tell who was praying in faith and who simply had faith in prayer. But when he died, the difference became clear. Those who had faith in prayer were confused and upset. They asked, “What was the point of all those prayers? How could God ignore such united, humble, heartfelt cries from His people? Where are His promises now? Does prayer even work? What good is it to believe in prayer at all?” But those who had prayed in faith saw things differently. Their faith was in God, not in the act of praying. They had asked for things they knew to be in God’s will—and those things had been granted. They had also asked for things that were uncertain, and in those cases they had left the outcome to God’s wisdom. When God answered, even if it was with a “no,” they were not shaken. They trusted that God had done what was best. Their prayers had been answered, and they were thankful. In fact, their praying in faith was strengthened, while those who only had faith in prayer felt disappointed. At first, the situation was dire. President Garfield’s shooting stirred deep fears across the nation. He had been attacked by someone claiming political motives, and his leadership was seen as essential for guiding the country away from corruption, greed, and division. His death, it was feared, would bring instability, bitterness, and even disaster. In that moment, the whole nation turned to God—pleading for help and healing. Both those who prayed in faith and those who only had faith in prayer were united in their desperate cries for mercy. And those prayers were answered. President Garfield’s life was extended for a time. Again and again, when death seemed certain, God answered the prayers of His people, and the shadow of death was pushed back so that the President might remain alive to serve a purpose still unfolding. Meanwhile, the very things that believers were right to ask for were being granted by God through His wise providence. Political strife quieted around the President’s sickbed. The nation’s moral awareness was stirred, especially regarding the selfish pursuit of office and betrayals of public trust. A shared sorrow united the country—breaking down barriers of party and region—and hearts that had once been divided were brought together in compassion and common hope. More than that, the crisis awakened a fresh sense of dependence on God, drawing people to Him. A renewed religious spirit spread across the land, and many were moved to pray—offering visible proof that the heart of the nation still longed for God in its time of need. During those weeks, the President’s designated successor gained the sympathy and confidence of the people. He was being shaped by the trial into a man more prepared for the role he might have to assume at any moment. The disaster so greatly feared in the early days was held back through the faithful prayers of God’s people. And only when the President’s death would no longer threaten the country with deep harm—only then was he allowed to die. Still, many asked: “But all those prayers were offered for the life of the President—and yet, in the end, his life was taken. How can this be reconciled with God’s promises to hear the united prayers of His children?” The answer is clear: God's people had the right to pray in faith for the President’s life insofar as that life was essential to the well-being of the nation. Within that boundary, they could be confident God would hear and grant an answer of peace. But anything beyond that—whether his continued life would be best for him, or best for the country in the long run—was not theirs to know. That was God’s alone to judge. In those matters, true prayer in faith leaves the final decision with God. When danger, judgment, or national disaster is near, we may absolutely pray in faith that God would forgive and spare. And in this case, He did. The peril passed, the nation was preserved, the people were drawn together, and God was glorified. But when the question turns to whether one man’s life should be prolonged further—whether that would be better for the nation or for the man himself—that question belongs to God alone. Every sincere believer praying in faith would admit: “I don’t know what is best, Lord—but You do. Do what You know is good.” And such a prayer is always answered—because no prayer can truly be offered in faith if it does not entrust to God the outcome of what only He can see fully. When we need wisdom or strength to fulfill our duty—whether in spiritual or daily matters—we can pray with full confidence that God will answer, because the need is clear, and His promise is sure. But we cannot say with certainty that another’s life must be spared in order for us to do God’s will. That’s not ours to decide. We have no right to demand that life continue—only the right to ask that it remain until its removal would no longer cause true harm to us or to the one who lives. And when we pray that way—in trust, not demand—and God still chooses to take that life, we can be confident it was for the best. Always. Some people said, “The prayer-test has failed. Here was the perfect chance for God to prove the power of prayer—and He didn’t take it. Now the skeptics will mock, and many who once believed in prayer will stop believing.” But do we really think God made a mistake at that moment? Was His cause less important to Him than to us? Did He not fully understand what was at stake? Of course not. The failure was not God’s—it was in people’s mistaken belief that a public miracle would prove God’s power and cause faith to rise. But all of God’s wonders in the past—plagues in Egypt, the parting of the Red Sea, manna in the wilderness—did not bring lasting faith to Israel. And if He repeated such wonders today, they would not produce true faith now either. People still repeat the same error: thinking that if only a sign or miracle were given, the unbelieving would become believers. Yet Jesus said that those who refuse to listen to Moses and the prophets will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead. It was good for our mistaken faith in prayer to be shaken, so that it might be replaced by a true faith in God. The error of many scientists who mocked prayer was in believing that Christians claimed some mystical force in prayer itself. So, they asked for a scientific test of this supposed power. But prayer is not something to be tested—God is the one to be trusted. God invites people to test Him, not a method or formula. Those who looked to Garfield’s recovery as proof of prayer’s power were disappointed. God refused to become a player in a human argument or a personal dispute. To any demand for a sign, God gives the same answer He gave in Jesus’ day: “A wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign; and no sign will be given but the sign of the prophet Jonah.” Jonah proclaimed judgment, the people of Nineveh repented and prayed, and God spared them. So too, when judgment seemed to hang over America with the attempted assassination of President Garfield, people cried out to God—and God answered by averting disaster. A new generation received the same sign as Nineveh. Around the world, countless people recite prayers without understanding them. The most frequently used form of prayer today is repeated by millions of Buddhists, though many do not know what it means. The phrase “Om mani padme hum” is recited endlessly. Two syllables are just meaningless sounds, the other four a metaphor whose meaning is long forgotten. These prayers are even written on scrolls or prayer wheels, spun by water or machines to generate blessing. This is faith in prayer—not prayer in faith. There is no true praying in such mechanical, mindless repetition of words not even understood. Muslims, especially among the dervishes, often view prayer as both inherently meritorious and powerful. They spend long hours calling out to God, convinced that sheer repetition will bring blessing. And sadly, this same mindset exists among many Christians. How often do we hear someone say of a rebellious son, “Oh, he must be saved—he’s the child of many prayers”? Or, “His mother’s prayers cannot have been in vain—they must be answered.” The confidence is not in the mother’s faith, but in the mother’s prayers—regardless of whether they were full of trust or simply desperate sorrow. This is not the kind of prayer God promises to answer. During his time as an army chaplain, the writer once urged a reckless, immoral soldier to consider his soul. The man responded quickly, “Oh, Chaplain, I think more about those things than you might think. I’ve got a praying mother, and I’ve got great faith in her prayers. When I’m in a tough fight and men are dying around me, I often feel like her prayers are keeping me alive. I’ve got strong faith in those prayers.” Then he took another drink of whiskey—and not long after, he died from delirium tremens right in front of his colonel’s tent. He showed no sign of repentance, nor did he offer even a single prayer in faith. His “faith” was sentimental—faith in prayer—but not faith in God. Jesus didn’t say, “Have faith in prayer.” He said, “Have faith in God.” Then He went on to say: “Whoever says to this mountain, ‘Be taken up and thrown into the sea,’ and does not doubt in his heart but believes that what he says will come to pass—it will be done for him. Therefore I say to you, whatever you pray and ask for, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours.” That promise has never failed the disciple who truly prays in faith. But it has confused and disappointed many who simply had faith in prayer. The promise still stands just as firm today as when Jesus spoke it. Whatever a child of God asks for in real faith—resting in God’s wisdom and goodness—will be granted in line with that faith. Faith in God never misleads or fails. But faith in prayer—faith in the act or formula—often does fail, as it should.

item.title: Christian - You Wrote Your Will, What Does It Look Like?
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
John Harris

Perhaps you comfort yourself with the thought that you will give generously at death. A life of giving that ends with a large gift in your will may look like a beautiful sunset after a fine day. But no gift made at death can excuse a lifetime of greed or remove its shame.

If it is a serious sin to delay turning to God until your final hour, how can it be harmless to delay one of the main duties of the Christian life until then? Even if you ordered a great charity to be built over your grave, it would still stand as a monument to covetousness. Its inscription could honestly read: “Death’s victory over greed.” For the person who withholds generosity until the end is not so much offering a gift to God as yielding what he can no longer keep.

You are also going against God’s providence and clear warnings. Future generosity depends on uncertain events. Richard Baxter once said he had saved a thousand pounds and, having no children, intended to give it to charity. Before he could do so, the king closed the exchequer, and he lost the money. He shared this story to urge others: if you want to do good, do it quickly and with all your strength.

Yet by making charity mainly a matter of your last will, you are depending on something that has become a symbol of uncertainty.

What you plan to postpone until your natural death, the true Christian does when he “dies to sin.” At that moment he gives himself—and all he owns—to God. And he has this great advantage: he becomes his own executor. He enjoys the Godlike privilege of personally doing for God what you intend to leave for others to do after you are gone.

You say you are only God’s steward and that God is the true owner of your property. Yet instead of using it faithfully for God’s glory and giving regular account, your greed makes it necessary for death to remove you from your position, so that what you possess will not remain unused forever.

Your Lord tells you to use worldly wealth to gain eternal friends, so that when you fail, they may welcome you into everlasting dwellings. The generous Christian may expect a joyful welcome. But what welcome can you expect? If there is any sense of relief connected with your arrival, it would be that death has at last loosened your grip on your possessions—and even that would feel like a silent rebuke.

When your Lord comes to receive what is his—with increase—you will not be able to point to multiplied talents. Any increase will only begin after you are gone, because only then will your possessions be put to better use. In that case, you would stand like the unprofitable servant.

You are reversing God’s order. Instead of your death being mourned as a loss to Christ’s cause, you are placing yourself among those whose death is counted a gain. Those who might otherwise pray that your life be prolonged for the church’s benefit could be tempted to wish for your removal.

If your example became common, what would Christ’s cause lose if half the Christian world were taken away? Your practice so opposes God’s design that such a loss—now unthinkable—would seem almost necessary for the work of Christ on earth to move forward.

One question still remains. If generosity delayed until death is a poor substitute for generosity in life, then how much do you truly intend to leave to charity in your will?

We know this is finally a matter between you and God. Your decision must take into account circumstances you understand better than anyone else. Still, in most cases, an honest and consistent Christian can judge more clearly what should be given to mercy than the person making the will can judge about himself.

Should you not at least suspect this may be true of you? Does not your present lack of generosity toward Christian causes make it reasonable to fear that what you have left to them in your will is too small? And even if it is small, it is still your decision. It is your will.

When people write a will, they usually remember those they love most. You say that Christ is your greatest friend. Yet you are willing to offer him only a small and reluctant gift—and that is your choice.

You make your will knowing that the world is full of suffering. Much more of what you own could help relieve that suffering. But you choose not to direct it that way.

You also prepare your will while hoping soon to enter perfect joy. You imagine that while others are discovering how you distributed your property, your soul will be sharing in the happiness of the righteous in heaven. Yet you are content that almost none of that joy will come from having used your wealth faithfully—and that too is your choice.

Remember, this is not a passing thought or a careless mistake. It is a deliberate act. You even begin by declaring that you are “of sound mind.” In other words, this decision expresses your settled judgment—the ruling intention of your heart.

After withholding your property from Christ’s cause during life, you then take careful steps to continue that withholding after death. You do so knowingly and willingly, and you confirm it with the strongest authority you possess: your will.

Yes—this is the document on which you expect to rest your dying head. Your will—part of what you call your preparation for death. Your will—drawn up, as you suppose, so that the matter of your property will not disturb you at the end, and so that you may think of it with peace.

Your will is written partly to prepare you for the moment when you will hear, “Give an account of your stewardship.” You are drafting it on your way to the judgment seat, where one of the first questions will be how you used the talents God placed in your hands.

Professing Christian, take this seriously. No one can say what your deathbed might have been like if your attention had never been drawn to this issue. But now that your conscience has been warned, if you leave your will unchanged, do not be surprised if your final pillow feels like it is filled with thorns.

Correct this wrong at once by increasing what you leave to works of mercy. Better still, act as your own executor: begin doing good now, and enjoy the deep satisfaction of it. Best of all—if you are able—do both.

item.title: White Queen of The Cannibals: The Story of Mary Slessor of Calabar
item.dc:creator: maryslessor
item.content:encoded: This free Christian audiobook is of, "White Queen of The Cannibals: The Story of Mary Slessor of Calabar" written by A.J. Bueltmann. When Mary was young, she heard her mother read about the dangers and rewards of missionary work in Calabar, Africa. This challenged Mary Slessor's young heart and she determined to serve her Lord there. White Queen of the Cannibals records her courage as a missionary to the worst of pagans. The story is simply told that it might inspire children to Christian service.

item.title: Wasted Faith
item.dc:creator: jimelliff
item.content:encoded:
“What is most alarming is the risky willingness of many professing Christians to gamble eternity on an emotional one-time experience, a ‘sinner’s prayer’ properly prayed, or a feeling of substantial relief at a juncture in time, without ever taking a serious look at what is evident now, at this moment. Is eternal life of so little value that it seems unnecessary to examine ourselves for the evidence of it?” — from Wasted Faith
This audiobook has been put up by permission of the author.

item.title: In Light of Eternity: The Life of Leonard Ravenhill
item.dc:creator: macktomlinson
item.content:encoded:
"Leonard Ravenhill was a man who was centered on prayer. His wife once told a close friend that Leonard regularly spent eight hours a day in prayer. This inward passion for God caused an outward cry for truth and holiness. He preached with a passion for revival all over the world, but especially in the United States, where he was especially burdened about what appeared to him to be a generally lukewarm "church". He longed to see a mighty move of God in his day, and this passion came out in his preaching."
Note: This audiobook was made with the permission of its author Mack Tomlinson.

item.title: Chundra Lela: From Indian Priestess To Christian Evangelist
item.dc:creator: adalee
item.content:encoded:
Other Books About Chundra Lela

  • Chundra Lela: The Story of A Hindu Devotee and Christian Missionary by Rev. Z. F. Griffin, 1911
  • A Pilgrim of India, from Comrades In Service by Margaret Burton, pp. 25-44
Additionally, you can find a summary from Walker and Lunt, Talks on Races to Be Won and Wilmot, The Playing of the Moonbeams.

Chundra Lela: A Convert From Hinduism

“You wicked creature! It’s your sin that caused this!” These harsh words were directed at a sobbing nine-year-old girl, Chundra Lela, as her earrings were ripped from her ears and her jewels torn from her hair. Even as the daughter of a priest to the Rajah (King) of Nepal, she endured the suffering and disgrace assigned to widows in India. Chundra Lela had been married at the age of seven to a boy of nine. When news came of his death, her future turned bleak. She knew no one would care for or love her. By the time she reached fifteen, she had read many Hindu sacred texts, all of which claimed widowhood was the result of great sin. They also offered hope—pardon could be found through a pilgrimage to four great temples located in the north, south, east, and west of the land, a journey of 3,000 miles. Desperate, she decided this was her only path to peace. Without telling her family, she confided in two maids she trusted, asking them to join her on the pilgrimage to seek forgiveness. They agreed, and one quiet night, the three girls stole away to begin their journey.

The Pilgrimage Begins

The journey took them to the temple of Jaganath in Puri on India’s east coast. From there, they traveled south to the Ramanath temple on an island between India and Ceylon. Next, they headed west to the temple of Krishna at Dwarka. Finally, they crossed the scorching plains of northern India to the Ganges River, climbing the Himalayas to reach the temple of Vishnu at Badrinath. The pilgrimage lasted seven years. Along the way, Chundra Lela bathed in sacred rivers, worshiped at countless shrines, and offered sacrifices to idols. However, even after fulfilling the requirements for pardon, she still felt the weight of sin and dissatisfaction.

A Life of Self-Torture and Devotion

Determined to find peace, Chundra Lela embarked on more pilgrimages. She became a Sadhu, a holy person, vowing to endure self-torture for three years. She smeared herself with ashes and sat in the scorching sun with five fires burning around her during the summer. At midnight, she stood on one foot before an idol, praying until dawn. During the cooler months, she spent her nights submerged in a pond with water up to her neck, praying continuously. She endured this night after night, repeating the names of gods using her beads. She later said, “Nobody knows how long those nights were or how much I suffered before morning.” Despite all her efforts, Hinduism failed to bring her peace of mind.

Chundra Lela Hears God's Word

One day, while visiting a friend’s home, Chundra Lela noticed her reading a strange book. Curious, she asked, “What are you reading?” Her friend explained that it was the Bible, given to her by a missionary. Chundra Lela quickly obtained her own copy and began reading. For the first time, she learned of a Savior who offered forgiveness for sins. Eager for more, she sought instruction from the missionaries and soon embraced Christianity. The same zeal she had shown in her Hindu practices, she now devoted to spreading the message of Christ.

A Christian Missionary's Journey

Chundra Lela immediately began sharing the gospel, taking the Bible with her and preaching from house to house and street to street. She worked tirelessly, often skipping meals and rest. After two years, she decided to return to the places where she had once worshiped idols as a Sadhu. This time, she preached Christ at those same shrines. Her Christian pilgrimage lasted several years as she traveled through Assam and Sylhet, even visiting a queen who had shown her kindness during her time as a Sadhu. In the palace, she read the Bible and preached about Jesus.

Chundra Lela's Final Days

As she grew older, the missionaries offered to build her a house in a quiet spot outside the city. However, Chundra Lela insisted that the house be built on the roadside. She explained, “When I am too old to walk, I want to be able to crawl to the door and preach to the people as they pass by.” Her wish was granted, and until her final days, she continued to speak to the passing crowds about Christ, the Savior who had redeemed her and brought peace to her soul.
