item.title: The Christmas Pudding That Made an Atheist Believe
item.dc:creator: mildredcable
item.content:encoded:
The following story shows how deeply the children absorbed the spirit of faith that filled the home. For them, praying and watching for God’s answer was simply part of life. But not everyone in the house was so easily convinced. Lydia, the servant maid, thought this “faith business” was a poor foundation for managing a household—even though she had to admit there was always food on the table and her wages were never late.

Lydia was fiercely loyal to Miss Soltau, though in a dramatic and emotional way. She longed to give her mistress the kind of luxuries and comforts that clearly weren’t part of God’s plan. One evening, while carrying supper to a sick child, Lydia made her feelings known. The little one had begun chatting cheerfully about Christmas and the wonderful puddings they would soon enjoy.

“There ain’t going to be no pudding,” Lydia said bluntly. “Miss Soltau’s too poor to buy currants and raisins.”

Though startled, the child quickly rallied and caught Lydia off guard.

“Lydia,” she said, “let’s have a secret. When you bring my tray at night, we’ll pray for currants—and see if they come. Don’t let’s tell anyone.”

Lydia eagerly agreed. The idea suited her perfectly—she would be part of a private plan to supply the family’s needs. And if currants did come (and they often did, somehow), she could feel proud of her role in the miracle.

So the two prayed faithfully—but no currants came. As Christmas neared, there was still no sign of an answer.

Then, one night, Miss Soltau returned home near midnight after tending her sick mother. At the door, an excited Lydia met her, breathless.

“Please, ma’am, there’s a parcel come, and I think it’s the currants! The man just put it down and went away.”

Miss Soltau, used to Lydia’s flair for drama, calmed her.

“I’m expecting parcels for China,” she said. “Leave it in the hall and go to bed. You should have been there long ago.”

She went into her study to write some letters. But soon, Lydia appeared again—this time in tears.

“Oh, Miss, please open the parcel. I can’t sleep till I know if it’s the currants.”

Miss Soltau, unaware of the secret prayer pact, was ready to deal firmly with Lydia’s outburst. But amid her sobs, Lydia blurted out the whole story. Together, they opened the parcel—and found twenty pounds of currants. Now both were thrilled. They agreed to say nothing until the children were gathered for evening prayers. Then the bale would be revealed.

The little sick girl, bundled in her dressing gown, was carried downstairs. A cheer of joy filled the room when the children saw the pile of dried fruit. Lydia beamed with pride, knowing she had played a part. Her young prayer partner, with a mixture of awe and certainty, clapped her hands and said:

“You see, Lydia, I knew God would send it.”

After the laughter and thanksgiving, Miss Soltau reminded them gently, “A pudding can’t be made from currants alone.” So together, they prayed—listing before their Heavenly Father the ingredients still missing: flour, sugar, spice, suet, raisins, and more.

The days until Christmas were few. Each afternoon, the children came running in from school asking:

“Has the pudding come yet?”

Just before Christmas, it did. During evening prayers, as Miss Soltau was reading a Psalm, a knock came at the door. Charlie jumped up and said:

“Maybe it’s the pudding!”

Lydia went to open the door and returned dragging a large hamper. Charlie cried out:

“Put away the Bibles—it’s the pudding!”

The room erupted in joyful noise as they opened the basket. Inside was everything needed: flour, sugar, suet, raisins, spices—and even oranges and cakes for everyone. Lydia, now fully convinced, declared herself a firm believer in the power of prayer. From that day forward, she never again doubted the foundation of faith that upheld the household.

There was no clue to the sender. The delivery man simply said:

“There is no name, and no answer.”

There were many times when no clue ever came about who had sent a gift. But in this case, the mystery was solved five months later.

One evening, Miss Soltau was struggling through the dark, fighting a strong southwest wind to reach the pillar-box. There, she saw a woman—frail and elderly—also trying to post a letter. Though Miss Soltau recognized her as someone from Wellington Square, she didn’t know her name. Seeing her struggle against the wind, Miss Soltau offered to walk her home. The woman gratefully accepted.

As they walked, the lady asked, “Aren’t you the one who lives with all the little children?”

“I am,” Miss Soltau replied. “Their parents are missionaries.”

The woman brightened with interest. “Then I should take this chance to apologize for sending a bale of currants without any note. My husband has relatives in Spain who sent him a large shipment of dried fruits last Christmas—far more than we could ever use. We often watch your children running off to school, and I said to him, ‘That’s the kind of household that could use a bale of currants.’ So, we sent them over.”

“You can hardly imagine the joy they brought to my little family,” Miss Soltau said.

The stormy wind interrupted the rest of the conversation, but the woman managed to explain further. After sending the currants, she had felt moved to supply everything else the children would need for a proper Christmas treat—so the second parcel had followed.

“You must forgive me,” she said, “for the way it was done. Not knowing you personally, I wasn’t sure how to send a gift to the children—so I sent it anonymously. But now that we’ve met, I do hope to call on you soon.”

Before they parted, Miss Soltau asked if the children might be allowed to come and thank their kind benefactors in person. The next day, dressed in their best clothes and each having memorized a Scripture verse, the children walked across the square to pay their visit. They were ushered into the room where Mr. Kay, an invalid, lay resting on a sofa.

He listened with deep interest as Miss Soltau shared her side of the story. He was visibly moved by the sight of so many children who, at such little cost to himself, had received such great joy. As he asked about each child’s parents, he heard stories of remarkable devotion and sacrifice for the sake of the Gospel.

Once the ice was broken, the children couldn’t stop talking. They told him about little Emma and her struggle with rheumatic fever, about Lydia’s blunt disbelief, and about the secret prayer for currants—and the surprising answer that had come.

Mr. Kay was shaped by the rationalist thinking of the 19th century, where science was often seen as standing in opposition to belief in divine providence. In those days, the lines were clear—many who rejected Christianity openly called themselves atheists. Mr. Kay had placed himself firmly among the free-thinkers.

Yet now, lying there, he looked at Miss Soltau—her face glowing with a heavenly light—and at the children who so naturally viewed him as a messenger from God. In the background, he pictured their parents, living with courage and faith on the far side of the world.

And in that moment, the walls of unbelief he had built began to collapse. The man who had once said in his heart, “There is no God,” found himself overcome by a Presence he could not deny. For the first time in his life, he bowed inwardly before the God he had long rejected.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Christmas Pudding That Made an Atheist Believe

item.title: Struggling to Find a Text to Preach!
item.dc:creator: chspurgeon
item.content:encoded:
From: Charles Spurgeon Autobiography, Chapter 19 - The Boy-preacher of Fens

In my earlier years, I came across a statement in a book of lectures on preaching that deeply troubled me. It said something like this: “If a man struggles to choose a text, he should go back to the shop or the farm—he clearly lacks the ability to be a minister.” Since I often found choosing a text to be a heavy burden, I began to wonder if I should give up preaching and take up some other kind of work. But I have not done so, because I still firmly believe that God has clearly confirmed my calling, even if that lecturer’s harsh words seemed to disqualify me.

That remark weighed so heavily on my conscience that I went to my grandfather—who had been preaching for fifty years—and asked if he ever had trouble picking a subject. He answered honestly that it had always been his greatest difficulty, and that preaching itself was no trouble in comparison. I remember his words: “The problem isn’t that there aren’t enough texts, but that there are so many. I feel torn between them.” We are like a lover of fine flowers, surrounded by the best in the garden, told to pick just one. He lingers between the rose and the lily, unable to choose just one from among thousands of beauties.

I still find this to be true. Choosing a text is a real struggle for me—not because there’s a lack, but because there’s an abundance. The French call it embarras de richesse—an embarrassment of riches. It’s not confusion from having too little, but from having too much—so many truths calling for attention, so many duties to preach, and so many spiritual needs to address.

Often I spend long hours in prayer and waiting just to find the right subject—that is the biggest part of my preparation. I’ve worked hard over many ideas, pondered points of doctrine, shaped outlines from verses, and then buried those outlines as if they never existed. I drift for hours, as if lost at sea, until at last I see the guiding light and can set sail directly toward the goal. I believe that almost every Saturday I come up with enough outlines to last me a month—if I felt free to preach them. But I dare not use them, any more than an honest sailor would bring illegal goods to shore.

I am always sure to have the most happy day when I get a good text in the morning from my Master. When I have had to preach two or three sermons in a day, I have asked Him for the morning subject, and preached from it; and I have asked Him for the afternoon’s topic or the evening’s portion, and preached from it, after meditating on it for my own soul’s comfort — not in the professional style of a regular sermon-maker, but feasting upon it for myself. Such simple food has done the people far more good than if I had been a week in manufacturing a sermon, for it has come warm from the heart just after it has been received in my own soul; and therefore it has been well spoken, because well known, well tasted, and well felt. Sometimes, my texts have come to me in a very remarkable way. While I was living at Cambridge, I had, as usual, to preach in the evening at a neighboring village, to which I had to walk. After reading and meditating all day, I could not meet with the right text. Do what I would, no response came from the sacred oracle, no light flashed from the Urim and Thummim; I prayed, I searched the Scriptures, I turned from one verse to another, but my mind would not take hold of a text; I was, as Bunyan would say, “much tumbled up and down in my thoughts.” Just then, I walked to the window, and looked out. On the other side of the narrow street in which I lived, I saw a poor solitary canary bird upon the slates, surrounded by a crowd of sparrows, who were all pecking at it as if they would tear it to pieces. At that moment the verse came to my mind, “Mine heritage is unto me as a speckled bird, the birds round about are against her.” I walked off with the greatest possible composure, considered the passage during my long and lonely walk, and preached upon the peculiar people, and the persecutions of their enemies, with freedom and ease to myself, and I believe with comfort to my rustic audience. The text was sent to me, and if the ravens did not bring it, certainly the sparrows did.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Struggling to Find a Text to Preach! - Charles Spurgeon

item.title: From London Parlors to Newgate’s Cells: Elizabeth Fry’s Bold Mission
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: The following is Chapter 10 of Twelve Wonderful Women by E. H. Farrance. Please note that we do not yet have an audiobook of this title on our site. During a visit to London as a young girl, Elizabeth Gurney met an elderly friend, Deborah Darby. Upon seeing the sincere and thoughtful girl, Deborah said she would one day be “a light to the blind, speech to the mute, and feet to the lame.” These words deeply moved Elizabeth and were later fulfilled in remarkable ways. Elizabeth Gurney was born in Norwich on May 21, 1780, one of ten children. Her parents were Quakers. Her father was a banker, known for his broad mind and sharp intellect. Her mother was a well-educated and refined woman, whose death left a deep sorrow in Elizabeth’s early life. At a yearly Quaker meeting, the preaching of William Savery stirred Elizabeth’s heart. She began to grow tired of her life of comfort and fun, and started to long for something deeper. She gave up music, dancing, and outward finery, and chose to wear the plain clothing of strict Quaker tradition. In 1800, Elizabeth married Joseph Fry, a junior partner in a bank. They lived at St. Mildred’s Court in London, which also served as the place of business. Her life quickly became full and busy. The Frys kept an open home for guests. As time went on, children were born. With a large household to manage, guests to care for, frequent letters exchanged with her sisters, and set times of quiet reflection, her days were full. She was appointed by the Quakers to visit the school and workhouse in Islington. Yet, she still felt restless. She longed for a greater field of service, for more direct work among the poor. After Joseph’s father passed away, they moved to the family’s old and lovely home in Plashet, Essex. After the noise and bustle of London, the peace and beauty of the countryside brought her great joy. God was gently guiding His servant, as He always does when His faithful children long to follow His will. In 1813, at the request of a friend, Elizabeth Fry made her first visit to Newgate Prison, accompanied by the sister of Sir Fowell Buxton. At that time, the condition of prisons was terrible. Everyone was kept together—young and old, criminals, people with mental illness, debtors, and even children. There was hardly any bedding, and no coal or candles were provided. The women were wild and desperate, the air was foul, and the level of misery and sin was beyond description. Alcohol was sold freely to anyone with money. The entire system was full of bribery and corruption, and many of the jailers were no better in character than the prisoners. When Elizabeth and her friend arrived, they found about three hundred women, many with children, crowded into filthy rooms. There was no order or work provided, and they were all under the care of just a man and his son. The women cooked, washed, and slept on the floor. In spite of how dreadful the place was, Elizabeth Fry and her friend went into the cells and spoke kindly to the women. Her compassionate heart was deeply moved, and she longed to do something lasting to help them. The second time Elizabeth Fry visited Newgate, she asked to be left alone with the women, and her request was granted. She read to them, spoke about the love of the Saviour, and then asked for their help in starting a school for the children. She was deeply moved by their response and their clear desire to improve life for their children. For a time, illness and sorrow in her own family kept her from returning, and all she could do was send regular gifts of clothing to the poor prisoners. In 1817, Mrs. Fry resumed her work. The women in the prison welcomed her with tears of joy when she offered to teach their children. They even chose one of their own to lead the school. Elizabeth and a faithful group of helpers gave themselves fully to the work, though the City Magistrates strongly discouraged them. Still, the effort was made, and the results were remarkable: no longer was the prison filled with wild, half-dressed, drunken women demanding charity. Instead, it became quiet—no longer echoing with foul language, curses, or immoral songs. Between 1818 and her final illness in 1841, Elizabeth Fry—except for one occasion—visited every ship that left England carrying women convicts to Botany Bay, seeking to ease their harsh conditions. Her work was praised in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords. It was demanding work, and her daughters often helped her manage the many letters she received from across the country concerning her efforts at Newgate. She also endured much personal hardship and pain in her struggle to bring an end to capital punishment for the crime of forgery. Here is an example of Elizabeth Fry’s remarkable influence. Years later, a woman in Sydney confessed that during one of Mrs. Fry’s talks—what she called a sermon—she had tried not to listen by silently counting to herself. As Mrs. Fry was saying goodbye to the prisoners, she called this young woman aside. Placing her hand on the woman’s shoulder, she said slowly and firmly, “Let not thine eyes covet.” She said nothing more. “Her voice was quiet and solemn; kind like a mother, yet firm like a judge.” When the young woman arrived in the colony, she behaved well for a time. But one day, while looking into her mistress’s box, a gold thimble caught her eye. She slipped it onto her finger at once. Just as she was about to close the lid, she suddenly felt Mrs. Fry’s hand on her shoulder again—the same gentle, firm touch. She looked around in fear, but found herself alone. Overwhelmed, she put the thimble back and trembled with fright. The secret of Mrs. Fry’s influence lay in her deep sense of God’s presence. She often wrote in her journal, “Thou hast been my stronghold, my Rock and my Fortress.” Though she worked tirelessly to improve the prisoners’ outward conditions, she never failed to share with them the message of salvation through the death of the Lord Jesus Christ. A friend who once accompanied Mrs. Fry to Newgate and witnessed her reading the Scriptures to the women wrote this account: “After a few minutes of silent prayer, she quietly opened the Bible and turned to Isaiah chapter 53. Her manner was deeply reverent. She read with such clear, gentle, and well-measured speech that not a word was lost. As she read, her mind seemed fully focused on the passage and nothing else. She spoke with gentle strength about the amazing love of God.” The response from the women was powerful. They deeply loved and respected her. Yet, despite the honor she received, Mrs. Fry remained truly humble, always praying for help and strength. In 1825 and 1826, the Fry family faced serious financial trouble and had to leave their home at Plashet. But the woman who had trusted God in good times now showed she could also trust Him in hardship and accept His will. Her prison reform efforts did not stop with Newgate. They spread to many towns across England and even into Ireland. She felt deeply when some of her children chose to join churches outside the Society of Friends. Yet, with gracious Christian love, she said: “If, after seeking to know God’s will—through His Spirit in your hearts and the teachings of Scripture—you believe you should join another church, then I bid you God-speed in this.” Elizabeth Fry was invited to meet Her Majesty, the young Queen, soon to be married to Prince Albert. She found the Queen to be interested and kind-hearted. Elizabeth continued to travel widely in connection with her work. Later, a season of long and painful illness came. But throughout it all, her faith and trust in God remained firm. Then the call came for this faithful servant, and she “passed away to be with Christ.” Elizabeth Fry was a woman who walked closely with the Lord each day. Because of that, she could hear His voice clearly and fully surrendered herself to His leading.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: From London Parlors to Newgate’s Cells: Elizabeth Fry’s Bold Mission

item.title: “I Killed My Own Children”: How the Gospel Ended Infanticide on One Island
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: Excerpt taken from: Woman’s Work in the Mission Field—or, The Special Ministry of Christian Women to the Heathen But the victories of the Gospel more than made up for the times of sorrow and discouragement. One by one, the cruel customs of heathen life were abandoned—among them, the terrible practice of infanticide. This was once common throughout Raiatea. Mrs. Williams employed a servant whose former role in society had been to kill infants. After her conversion to Christ, she became a new person—devoted, honest, faithful, and kind. She served Mrs. Williams and her children loyally for fifteen years. At times, with tears in her eyes, she would tell Mrs. Williams how she had once carried out these awful acts. Sometimes she would smother a newborn with a wet cloth. Other times she would pinch the infant’s throat until it died. In some cases, she would bury the baby alive under earth and stones deep in the forest. One of the most brutal methods she described was breaking the joints of the child—first the fingers and toes, then, if the baby lived, the ankles and wrists, then elbows and knees—and if it still lived, she would end its suffering by strangling it. She spoke of these past deeds with deep sorrow and repentance, as did many mothers who had taken the lives of their children before they came to know the truth. One woman admitted to killing sixteen children—all her own. Another confessed to nine, another to seven, and another to five. But after the Gospel came to Raiatea, this terrible sin was mourned with great grief. At one school celebration, where six hundred children stood before the king, something deeply moving happened. An elderly chief stood up, overcome with emotion, and cried out: “I must speak. Oh, if only I had known the Gospel was coming! If I had known these blessings were on their way, I would have saved my children. They would have been among this joyful group, repeating these precious words. But I destroyed them all. I have no children left. I was the father of nineteen, and every one of them is gone.” Then, turning to the king—his relative—he said with deep sorrow, “You, my brother, saw me kill child after child. But you never stopped me, never said, ‘Wait, brother! God is about to bless us; the Gospel is coming to our land.’” During Mr. and Mrs. Williams’ time in Raiatea, a young woman who served as a teacher in one of the missionary schools had an extraordinary story of being rescued from death years earlier. Her mother and father were of different social ranks in Raiatean society, and tradition required that all their children be put to death at birth. The first child was born and killed, and the second met the same fate—even though the father pleaded for its life. He clearly felt love for his children, and when their third child, a healthy baby girl, was born, he once again begged that she be allowed to live. But the mother and her relatives refused. While the father was away in the mountains, they buried the newborn alive. When he returned and learned what had happened, he rushed to the burial site, dug up the child, and found her still alive. He rescued her and gave her into the care of his brother and sister, who took the baby to Eimeo, an island about seventy miles away, where infanticide was not practiced. The father soon died and had never told the mother that their child had been saved. Some time later, Christianity reached Raiatea, and the mother became a believer. As her heart was awakened by the Gospel, she deeply mourned the children she had lost. Then one day, a woman revealed to her that one of her children had been spared. The mother immediately traveled to Eimeo—and there, to her overwhelming joy, she found her daughter alive and well. The girl had grown into a fine young woman, and she became her mother’s support and joy. She also became a sincere Christian and faithfully served as a teacher in the women’s school.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: How the Gospel Ended Infanticide on One Island

item.title: “I’m Half Alligator, Half Snapping Turtle!” — The Wildest Wife God Ever Changed
item.dc:creator: petercartwright
item.content:encoded:
Within the bounds of my district lived a local preacher, a small, kind-hearted, and pleasant man. He was known for his piety and was considered a good and useful preacher. However, his wife was his complete opposite in nearly every way—except that she was believed to be virtuous. She was high-tempered, domineering, quarrelsome, and fiercely opposed to religion.

She refused to prepare his clothes when he went out to preach and would not allow him to say a blessing at the table or pray with the family. When he tried to pray, she would cause as much disturbance as possible—turning over chairs while he read, sang, or prayed. If nothing else worked, she would even grab a cat and throw it in his face while he was kneeling in prayer.

The poor man was tormented nearly to the point of despair. He had invited several preachers to his home, hoping they could talk to her and calm her down, but it was all in vain. She cursed them to their faces and raged like a madwoman.

He had often urged me to visit, but to be honest, I was reluctant. I felt deep pity for him, as did everyone who knew his situation. But finally, after much insistence, I agreed to go home with him one evening, planning to stay the night.

As soon as we arrived, I could tell she was furious. It was as if the devil himself was in her, as big as an alligator. I made up my mind right then about how I would handle the situation.

After supper, her husband gently said, “Come, wife, stop what you’re doing and let us have prayer.”

At that, she exploded in anger. “I will have none of your praying around me!” she shouted.

I tried to speak to her calmly, reasoning with her, but the more I spoke, the angrier she became. She cursed me bitterly.

At that moment, I put on a stern look and said firmly, “Madam, if you were my wife, I would break you of your bad ways—or I would break your neck.”

“The devil I would!” she shouted. “You’re a fine Christian, aren’t you?” Then she let loose a volley of curses that were almost unbearable.

“Be still,” I said firmly. “We must and will have prayer.”

But she swore we would not.

“If you don’t calm down and behave yourself,” I warned, “I’ll put you out of doors.”

At this, she clenched her fists and swore, “I’m half alligator and half snapping turtle, and it’ll take a better man than you to put me out!”

We were in a small cabin, not far from the open door. Without hesitation, I grabbed her by the arm, swung her around in a circle, and sent her right out the door.

She sprang up, tore at her hair, and cursed with a fury I had rarely seen matched. The door was built strong to keep out hostile Indians, so I shut it tight, barred it, and went to prayer. Though my emotions were hard to describe, I was determined to win this battle or die trying.

Outside, she raged and screamed, but inside, I lifted my voice in a spiritual song, trying to drown her out. The five or six little children, terrified, ran and hid under the beds. Poor things—they were scared nearly to death.

I kept singing, and she kept raging, until finally, she exhausted herself and could hardly catch her breath. At last, she fell silent, then knocked at the door.

“Mr. Cartwright, please let me in,” she said.

“Will you behave yourself if I let you in?” I asked.

“Oh yes,” she answered.

Keeping my guard up, I opened the door, took her by the hand, and led her inside. She sat near the fireplace, drenched in sweat and pale as death.

After a moment, she sighed and said, “Oh, what a fool I am!”

“Yes,” I said, “one of the biggest fools I have ever seen in my life. And now, you must repent of this, or you will surely go to the devil in the end.”

She sat in silence.

Turning to the children, I said, “Come out now. Your mother won’t hurt you.” Then I looked at her husband and said, “Brother C., let us pray again.”

We knelt down, and both of us prayed. This time, she sat quietly, as gentle as a lamb.

And now, dear reader, though this was one of the hardest cases I had ever encountered, I must give glory to God. In less than six months after this battle with the devil, this woman was soundly converted. If ever there was a changed soul, it was hers. As her children grew, I believe all of them found salvation, and the entire family became a happy and godly household. She, who had once been so bold for the cause of the wicked one, became just as fearless in the cause of Christ.

Taken from Chapter 20 of Peter Cartwright's biography.

item.title: Satan: His Personality, Power and Overthrow
item.dc:creator: embounds
item.content:encoded: Why read a book on Satan? Because the Bible presents him not as a vague force but as a real, personal being whose character and methods shape the spiritual battlefield we all inhabit. Edward M. Bounds digs into Scripture to reveal Satan’s proud intelligence, relentless activity, and mastery of deception—showing him as the roaring lion stalking the faithful (1 Peter 5:8) and the ancient serpent whose power reaches into every aspect of human life. To ignore such an adversary is to leave one’s heart and mind defenseless; to understand him is to see the scope of his assaults and to grasp why Christ’s victory must be our hope and shield. Bounds draws on Peter, Jude, Paul, and the Gospels to equip believers with both sword and shield—the Word of God and the faith that quenches every fiery dart of the evil one. Here is practical counsel on prayer, watchfulness, holiness, and the full armor of God, all aimed at one end: to stand firm, resist the tempter, and live in the triumph of “the One who has bruised Satan under our feet” (Romans 16:20). This is no mere curiosity; it is survival and victory for every soul.
item.excerpt:encoded: Discover E.M. Bounds’ “Satan: His Personality, Power and Overthrow” and learn how Christ’s victory equips you to resist the tempter and stand firm.

item.title: The Story of Matula: A Congo Convert
item.dc:creator: johnbell
item.content:encoded:
Born into a world where superstition and fear ruled every moment, Matula’s journey from tribal outcast to bold evangelist stands as a testament to the life-changing power of the Gospel. Beaten, bound, and left for dead, he miraculously escaped his enemies and found refuge among the missionaries at Ngombe.
Through unimaginable trials—false accusations, brutal persecution, and the ravages of sleeping-sickness—Matula never wavered in his faith. His unwavering trust in God transformed not only his own life, but the hearts of chiefs, warriors, and villagers across the Lower Congo. Families were freed, schools founded, and entire towns kindled by his testimony.
This inspiring account, written by John Bell from firsthand interviews and mission records, invites you to witness one man’s courage, one community’s revival, and one divine promise fulfilled: that “My word will not return to me empty.”

item.title: The Mission of Sorrow
item.dc:creator: gardinerspring
item.content:encoded: Sorrow has a mission—not shaped by the wisdom of this world, but by the hand of God. Have you been laid low by illness, worn thin by long suffering, and warned by weakness of the grave? May the everlasting arms uphold you, and heaven’s light break through the shadows. Has the wife of your youth been taken? Have you watched, helpless, as a beloved child slipped beyond your reach? These are wounds that break the heart—but they are not beyond the reach of divine comfort. God sends sorrow not to sever, but to sanctify. He dims the light of this world to draw your eyes to a brighter one. His goal is not to weaken your bond to the ones you’ve lost, but to lift it upward—toward eternity, and toward Himself. Listen to author books by Gardiner Spring, such as, "The Attraction of the Cross" or "The Power of the Pulpit".

item.title: A Woman Who Laughed at Impossibility | Henrietta Soltau
item.dc:creator: mildredcable
item.content:encoded:
A Woman Who Laughed? What do you mean “laughed”? Henrietta Soltau didn’t laugh in humor—she laughed in steadfast confidence in her sovereign God, crying in prayer, “It shall be done!” When illness barred her from overseas missions, she refused to be deterred. Instead, she lifted her heart in prayer and praise, trusting in the Lord’s promise to sustain His work through every challenge—illness, poverty, storm, or strife.
Born into a devout Christian home where all nine Soltau children eventually bowed to Christ, Henrietta answered the call to serve through prayer at at home work, transforming Exeter’s poorest halls into thriving mission schools, founding Europe’s first training home for women missionaries, and carrying the Gospel through typhoons and civil unrest—all by the quiet power of prayer.

item.title: Humility: Adopting Paul’s “Less Than the Least” Mindset
item.dc:creator: isaacwatts
item.content:encoded:
Isaac Watts saw pride as a root‑poison in the soul and wrote these pages to help Christians dig it out by its very source. Here you will find no mere rules or moralizing, but a steady unfolding of:
  • Pride wears subtle disguises in every heart.
  • Christ’s humility is our pattern, and Paul’s self-abasement is our example.
  • There is quiet power in depending on God and knowing our nothingness.
  • Peace and grace flow from esteeming others and denying self.
Read slowly. Let each gentle conviction awaken you to your own need. Then, having learned to think “slightly less of yourself and slightly more of others,” discover the unsearchable riches Christ gives to the humble of heart.
A worm may lift itself up above other worms, but one step from a man crushes it into the dust. So it is with us. We may seem greater than our neighbors, but in God’s presence, we are nothing.
— Isaac Watts

item.title: They Who Comforted: Fanny Crosby & Agnes Weston
item.dc:creator: johnhawthorn
item.content:encoded: Discover the remarkable true stories of two 19th-century women whose faith and determination shaped history on both sides of the Atlantic. In America, Fanny Crosby—blind from infancy—became one of the most prolific hymn writers in history. Her soul-stirring lyrics—Blessed Assurance, Safe in the Arms of Jesus, and many more—offered comfort and hope to generations. Her physical blindness became the backdrop for a spiritual vision that illuminated hearts around the world. Meanwhile in Britain, Agnes “Mother” Weston devoted her life to the welfare of Royal Navy sailors. In a world of isolation and temptation, she established the Royal Sailors’ Rests—welcoming spaces that offered fellowship and gospel hope in place of alcohol. Her compassion and practical faith created a legacy of care that transformed countless lives. Though their paths were different—Crosby with her pen and hymns, Weston with her harbor of rest—both women shared a deep desire to serve God and uplift the weary. This dual biography reveals how two lives, surrendered to Christ, brought lasting hope and light to a darkened world.
item.excerpt:encoded: In America, Fanny Crosby—blind from infancy—became one of the most prolific hymn writers in history. Her soul-stirring lyrics—Blessed Assurance, Safe in the Arms of Jesus, and many more—offered comfort and hope to generations. Her physical blindness became the backdrop for a spiritual vision that illuminated hearts around the world.

item.title: The Triumph of John and Betty Stam
item.dc:creator: mrshowardtaylor
item.content:encoded:
In “The Triumph of John and Betty Stam” by Mrs. Howard Taylor you are about to listen to the lives of two young believers—John and Betty Stam—who answered God’s call to serve in inland China, trusting Him for every provision, caring for the sick, sharing the Gospel in mountain villages, and even welcoming their miracle baby into the world; but one winter dawn, as Red soldiers swept into their station and bound them for an unknown fate, they simply smiled and walked forward—what would become of them, and their precious child, in the days ahead?

The Triumph of John and Betty Stam, by Mary Geraldine Taylor, was first published in 1935 by the China Inland Mission. Public Domain Notice – searches of the U.S. Copyright Office renewal lists for 1962–1963 show no renewal registration for this work; under 17 U.S.C. § 304, the initial 28-year term expired and the text is now in the public domain. It may be copied, quoted, performed, narrated, and shared in any format under U.S. law without restriction or requirement for permission or royalties. item.title: "I Am Not Afraid!" | The Story of John W. Vinson Christian Martyr in China
item.dc:creator: ehhamilton
item.content:encoded: An inspiring portrait of John Vinson and his wife Jeanie’s unwavering faith and sacrificial devotion as they serve amid illness, hardship, and civil turmoil in early 20th-century China—culminating in Vinson’s courageous martyrdom and enduring legacy. Drawn from Jeanie’s heartfelt letters and firsthand accounts, their story bears witness to the power of steadfast love and quiet perseverance in the face of profound suffering.

item.title: The Backwoods Preacher: Peter Cartwright
item.dc:creator: petercartwright
item.content:encoded:
Bold, unflinching, and larger than life, Peter Cartwright blazed across America’s frontier with nothing but a Bible in one hand and unshakable faith in the other. From humble beginnings in the backwoods of Tennessee to thunderous camp meetings that drew thousands, Cartwright’s relentless energy and plainspoken conviction helped forge the spirit of early American Methodism—and left an indelible mark on a young nation.
In these pages you’ll ride alongside him through frontier settlements and burgeoning towns, witness his fiery clashes with skeptics and slaveholders, and share in the triumphs and tragedies that shaped his calling.
Rich with anecdotes of rattlesnake encounters, midnight baptisms, and the raw, unvarnished reality of life on the wild frontier, this is the unforgettable story of a man who stood toe to toe with the trials of his era—and whose legacy still echoes in churches across America. Prepare to be stirred, challenged, and ultimately transformed by the life of Peter Cartwright.
item.excerpt:encoded: Bold, unflinching, and larger than life, Peter Cartwright blazed across America’s frontier with nothing but a Bible in one hand and unshakable faith in the other.

item.title: Driven From Home: A Converted Jewess - Jeanette Gedalius
item.dc:creator: jeanettegedalius
item.content:encoded: Audiobook of Driven From Home: A Converted Jewess, Cast Out for Christ—The True Story of Jeanette Gedalius This is the true story of a Jewish girl who was saved at fifteen and, by nineteen, had already endured years of hardship for her faith. Converted as a teenager, she spent much of her youth away from home, suffering yet learning that “the cross is not greater than His grace.” But nothing could prepare her for the day the Rabbi came. Urging her mother to act before God’s wrath fell on their household, he stirred up fear and hostility. Pressured by the community—and even by those calling themselves Christians—her mother’s grief and rage erupted. One blow left her unconscious; when she awoke, she was thrust into the street, stripped of the home and family she had known since childhood. With no kind farewells, no loving embrace, and no blessing, she walked away—driven from home for the sake of “the hanged One.” In her deepest loneliness, she cried out to the God who had saved her, and He answered, proving once again that His love and faithfulness never fail.

item.title: Miraculous Healing: A Personal Testimony & Biblical Study
item.dc:creator: henryfrost
item.content:encoded: Miraculous Healing? Throughout his study the author resisted both extremes—on one side, outright denial of God’s power to heal; on the other, an uncritical embrace of every “sign and wonder” as if Scripture itself guaranteed health apart from any nuance. By carefully setting five cases of instantaneous, means-bypassing recoveries beside five equally well-attested cases where healing came through medicine or was withheld altogether, he drives home a vital principle: genuine faith submits to Scripture in full, not just to the passages we prefer. He warns that “Any biblical teaching can be misunderstood or twisted… especially when it appeals to personal interests.” Hence his constant appeal to both “scientific” reasoning and “spiritual” discernment, always testing experience against the Word—never allowing anecdote to overrule the broader teaching of Scripture, nor dogma to blind us to God’s compassionate work in individual lives. In doing so, he models the very balance he urges: trusting Christ “as the caring and compassionate Lord of the body” while exercising humble sobriety about what faith can claim and when it must rest in “Thy will be done.”

item.title: Living Water from the Fountain of Life
item.dc:creator: richardnewton
item.content:encoded:
The following is a free audiobook of sermons for children by Richard Newton: Living Water from the Fountain of Life: Simple Sermons Preached in The Children’s Church.

Richard Newton—whom Charles Spurgeon called “the prince of preachers among children”—was well known for his ability to communicate the truths of Scripture in a way that both children and adults could grasp. Each month, on the first Sunday afternoon, the congregation of St. Paul’s Church held a special service for children, known as The Children’s Church. During these services, the children would sit in the main area of the church, the regular order of worship was observed, and Newton would deliver a sermon prepared especially for them. What began as a hesitant experiment soon became one of the most fruitful gatherings of the church. The children listened with eager attention, and when questioned, they could clearly recall and explain the main points of the sermons. Remarkably, The Children’s Church drew more adults than any other afternoon service, as they too were edified by Newton’s clear and heartfelt preaching. The effort of simplifying sermons for children proved to be an added blessing to Newton himself, sharpening his ability to preach to “children of a larger growth.”

item.title: The Heroic In Missions: Pioneers In Six Fields
item.dc:creator: augustusbuckland
item.content:encoded: From Japan’s once-closed ports to Uganda’s fevered lakeshores, these six nineteenth-century narratives spotlight missionaries who traded comfort for Christ. Braving famine, fever, and hostile kings, pioneers such as Alexander Mackay and John Horden pressed on to translate Scripture, plant churches, and—when required—die for the gospel. First printed in 1894 and freshly revised, The Heroic in Missions is a compact gallery of patient, rugged faith that challenges today’s readers to carry the same steady light into the world’s dark places.
item.excerpt:encoded: From Japan’s once-closed ports to Uganda’s fevered lakeshores, these six nineteenth-century narratives spotlight missionaries who traded comfort for Christ.

item.title: A Devotional Study of Romans
item.dc:creator: bobjennings
item.content:encoded: The following commentary on Romans was written between 1992-1996 and has been compiled after the departure of Robert Jennings to Christ’s heavenly kingdom in November of 2012. We could not locate what Bob wrote on Romans 1:1-15; therefore, the book starts on Romans 1:16. Our desire is that this book will help you more clearly understand the Apostle Paul's letter to the Romans that contains the glorious truths about how in God's great wisdom He made a way to justify the sinner and still be just.

item.title: John Horden: 42 Years Among the Cree & Eskimo
item.dc:creator: augustusbuckland
item.content:encoded:
In 1851, schoolmaster and then missionary John Horden left Exeter with three weeks’ notice to bring the Gospel to Moose Factory—a tiny Hudson’s Bay Company post cut off by ice and forest. He and his wife endured storms that trapped their ship for weeks, then faced summers of swarming mosquitoes and winters plunging to forty-below as he mastered Cree, Ojibbeway and Eskimo in order to preach and translate Scripture in the language of the people.
Over forty-two years he rode dog-sled hundreds of miles, built churches, print-shops and schools with his own hands on that frozen frontier. Through famine, flood, epidemics and near-fatal rapids he remained “faithful unto death,” completing the first Cree Bible before his final illness.

item.title: Holiness—The False and the True: “Second Blessing” & “Higher Life” Examined
item.dc:creator: haironside
item.content:encoded:
Bit by bit, the light dawned: true holiness is not found by looking within, but by looking to Christ. In this clear, pastoral work, H. A. Ironside exposes the promise of the “Second Blessing” and “Higher Christian Life,” showing why any hope of holiness rooted in a special experience inevitably turns us inward—and away from the Savior.
With biblical simplicity, Ironside distinguishes our position in Christ from our changing condition. Justification, he insists, is not a feeling birthed in us but a verdict pronounced over us—God’s once-for-all declaration that the ungodly are righteous through faith in Jesus, who bore their judgment at the cross. Feelings may rise or fall; the Judge’s decision stands.
From that settled peace flows daily growth. The same grace that saves also keeps; the same Spirit who gives life empowers practical obedience. Holiness, then, is not a second ladder we climb but the fruit of resting in Christ’s finished work and walking by the Spirit.

item.title: Effective Praying: Meditations Upon The Subject of Prevailing Prayer
item.dc:creator: henryfrost
item.content:encoded:
Prayer is called "the key of paradise" and "the soul's blood," yet for many, it remains a source of frustration and mystery. We ask but do not receive. We speak but feel unheard. We wonder if our prayers have any real power.
In this timeless and profound exploration of Effective Praying, you will discover the foundational truths that transform prayer from a mere ritual into a dynamic, life-changing relationship with God. Going far beyond simple techniques, this guide illuminates the spiritual realities behind our conversations with the Almighty.
Inside, you will learn to:
1. Understand Prayer as Worship: Address the Father in the name of Christ and the power of the Holy Spirit.
2. Master the Four Elements of Prayer: Effectively practice supplication, petition, intercession, and thanksgiving.
3. Meet the Conditions for Answered Prayer: Align your heart with God's will and His divine principles.
4. Pray with Boldness and Faith: Move from uncertainty to a confident assurance that God hears you.
item.excerpt:encoded: Effective praying can change your life. Explore the foundational truths behind prayer and its spiritual impact.

item.title: From Ashamed to Unashamed: Cranmer’s Last Confession
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
In 1556, Mary and her Roman Catholic allies carried out a plan they had long been preparing. Mary had never forgiven Archbishop Cranmer for his role in her mother’s divorce or for his leadership in the Reformation. Though he had reluctantly supported the cause of Lady Jane Grey—making himself guilty of treason—this alone would likely have been pardoned. But the queen’s hatred and religious zeal were too deep to allow for mercy.

She had long intended to make Cranmer her victim. To satisfy both her own vengeance and the demands of Rome, she chose to prosecute him for heresy rather than treason. Cranmer was summoned by the Pope to appear in Rome and answer charges of heresy. But as he was being held in strict confinement at Oxford, he had no way to respond, even if he had wished to. As a result, the Papal Court condemned him as rebellious for failing to appear.

The queen then sent Bishop Bonner of London and Bishop Thirleby of Ely to Oxford to formally strip Cranmer of his office. The degradation ceremony was carried out with careful detail. Bonner, eager to humiliate Cranmer, went beyond what Thirleby could approve. At the end of it, Cranmer was dressed in a worn-out gown like that of a common countryman and a simple townsman’s cap, and then sent back to prison.

Cranmer was known for his gentle nature, but also for a natural timidity that he often had to fight against. His enemies knew this weakness well and decided to use it to disgrace him. After his trial at Oxford, he remained in close confinement, but was treated with more kindness than at any earlier time in his captivity.

He had longed to die with his friends, Latimer and Ridley, believing their example and company would strengthen and comfort him. But instead, he was forced to witness their martyrdom and left alone. Over time, the sense of abandonment—by both man and possibly even God—began to wear down his inner strength.

His captors noticed this change and planned to use it to make him deny his faith. As Archbishop of Canterbury and spiritual leader of the English Reformation, Cranmer’s recantation would be a powerful blow to Protestant believers. It would not only shame him but could also shake the faith of many others. The priests always intended to kill him, but they wanted him to go to death with the bitter knowledge that he had betrayed his faith for nothing.

They softened him with kind treatment, making him believe they truly wished to help him. In time, they convinced him that the queen and the Church would not only pardon him, but perhaps even restore him to favor—if he would sign a confession denying the truths he had once preached. Overcome by fear, the love of life, and horror at the tortures his friends had endured, Cranmer gave in during a moment of weakness. He signed a statement accepting the pope’s authority and the doctrine of Transubstantiation.

The Romanists, thrilled at their apparent victory, claimed he had surrendered completely. “Most likely,” writes Southey, “he signed an unclear or ambiguous recantation at first, and that the other five documents—where he supposedly endorsed doctrines he had often refuted and called himself a troublemaker and blasphemer—were forged under Bonner’s orders.” The whole situation was filled with doubt and confusion. What is certain is that Cranmer gave in, believing his life would be spared and that he could live quietly, without honor or wealth, wherever he wished.

A month passed after Cranmer’s recantation, during which he was left in uncertainty about his fate—even though it had already been decided. His enemies meant for this time to be filled with humiliation and inner torment, knowing that a man as pure and sensitive as Cranmer would feel deeply the weight of what he had done. And he did suffer—but that suffering, though bitter, brought spiritual healing.

During this time, Cranmer had the chance to reflect on his actions. He saw clearly how much damage his fear and denial could cause the Church. His conscience awakened, and by God's grace, he received the strength that his natural character lacked. His remorse was deep and painful, but it led to repentance rather than despair. Though he was not officially told of the Court’s plans, signs made it clear to him that he would be executed. He resolved to meet death with a courage born of faith.

On March 21, 1556, Cranmer was led out of prison to die. The usual sermon for such occasions was to have been preached at the stake, but because of wind and rain, it was delivered instead at St. Mary’s Church. The authorities fully expected Cranmer to repeat his recantation in front of the crowd, and the sermon was prepared accordingly.

Cranmer stood with solemn dignity. His face and bearing showed the deep sorrow and true repentance that filled his heart. Even his enemies felt pity. John Fox describes the scene: “Cranmer, during the sermon, revealed by his expressions more than words could say. At times he lifted his hands and eyes to heaven, then dropped them to the ground in shame. One could see in him the very image of heartfelt grief. More than twenty times tears streamed from his aged face. Those who were there testified they had never seen even a child weep so bitterly. The sight moved all who looked upon him, for in this old and honored man there was such a sorrowful countenance and such a flood of tears. Yet through it all, he remained calm and noble in his manner.”

Cranmer’s grief was not born of fear for his life, as his steady courage at the end clearly showed. It was the sorrow of deep repentance for denying his faith—a sorrow strong and pure, with no trace of weakness. He had conquered his fears. Had Queen Mary offered him life and freedom that very day in exchange for holding to his recantation, there is no doubt he would have refused, choosing instead to honor his Lord and seal his faith with his blood.

When it was his time to speak, Cranmer rose in the church and declared his submission to the queen and the laws of the land—but only in the sense of accepting whatever punishment they chose to inflict. His greater duty, he said, was to God, and that required him to speak the truth, not to deny the teachings God had entrusted to His Church.

“And now,” he said, “since I am come to the end of my life, upon which depends all that I have done and all that lies ahead—either to live with my Saviour Christ in joy, or to be forever tormented with the wicked in hell—and I see before my eyes either heaven”—and here he pointed upward—“or hell”—and pointed downward—“ready to receive me, I will declare to you my true faith, without disguise or deceit, for this is no time to dissemble.

"I believe in God the Father Almighty, Creator of heaven and earth; and in every part of the true Christian faith; in all that our Savior Jesus Christ taught, and in every word spoken by His apostles and prophets in the Old and New Testaments."

“And now I come to the thing that troubles my conscience more than anything I have ever said or done in my life,” Cranmer continued, “and that is the spreading of writings that went against the truth. I here renounce and reject them—those writings made by my own hand but contrary to what I truly believed in my heart. I wrote them in fear of death, hoping to save my life if it were possible. These include all the papers and statements I have signed since my degradation. In them I wrote many things that were false.

“And because my hand has sinned by writing against my heart, my hand shall be the first to suffer punishment. If I come to the fire, it shall be burned first. As for the Pope, I reject him utterly—as the enemy of Christ and Antichrist—with all his false teachings. And as for the Sacrament, I hold and believe just as I have taught in my book against the Bishop of Winchester.”

The Romanists were enraged by these words. Yet Cranmer’s heartfelt confession, clear and full of conviction, restored his honor in the eyes of many and confirmed his place as a faithful leader in the Reformed Church of England. The priests shouted him down and would not let him speak further. They hurried him away to the stake, where he met death with courage and faith, finishing his life as a true Christian and a steadfast witness for the truth.

Rome’s plan was defeated once again, and the cause of the gospel triumphed in the faith of its martyr. Just as Peter denied his Lord in weakness but later glorified Him in boldness, so Cranmer fell—but rose again, faithful to the end.

The martyrdom of Archbishop Cranmer dealt the greatest blow to the Roman Church of any execution during Mary’s reign. Thousands had witnessed his heartfelt repentance, and no statement from Bonner or the other clergy could convince them otherwise. Tens of thousands saw the calm courage with which he went to the stake. The entire nation grieved his death and was outraged by the cruelty shown to him. Even among those loyal to Rome, the event stirred horror.

Yet Cranmer’s blood only made his persecutors more ruthless. “Cranmer alone so far had been executed after a recantation,” wrote Froude. “To others, pardon had always been offered up to the last moment.” But now, even that small mercy was denied.

From the Cross and Crown, an upcoming audiobook.

item.title: Humility: The Beauty of Holiness
item.dc:creator: andrewmurray
item.content:encoded: Revised for readability in 2025, but not abridged. In an age that exalts self, Andrew Murray’s classic work points us to a better way: the beauty of humility before God. Written with piercing clarity and deep devotion, Humility: The Beauty of Holiness reminds us that the highest glory of the creature is to be nothing in itself, that God may be all. Murray shows that humility is not an optional virtue, but the very foundation of the Christian life. Drawing from Scripture and the life of Christ, he demonstrates how true holiness flows from lowliness of heart, and how only the empty can be filled with the fullness of God. Whether you are seeking renewal in your walk with Christ or longing for a deeper experience of His presence, this timeless book will challenge, convict, and comfort you with one central truth: the way down is the way up.

item.title: How God Awakens His People—Even Through Pain
item.dc:creator: horatiusbonar
item.content:encoded: The following is Chapter 9 from Horatius Bonar's book: The Night of Weeping (an audiobook that is coming soon). It may be long since the Holy Spirit first woke us from the sleep of death. Into that deep sleep we know we shall never fall again. The One who woke us will keep us awake until Jesus comes. In that sense, we will sleep no more. Yet much of our drowsiness remains. We are not fully awake, and often the heaviness of our old sleep returns. Living on the world’s enchanted ground, our eyes grow heavy, our senses confused, our conscience dull, and our strength weak. We fall asleep even at our watchtower, forgetting that the night is almost over and the day is near. While asleep, or half-asleep, everything goes wrong. Our steps are slow and lifeless. Our faith grows weak, our love grows cold, and our zeal fades. The freshness of earlier years is gone. Our boldness leaves us. Our plans are careless, and our work sluggish. The cause of Christ is hindered by us instead of helped. We weigh it down and harm it. But God will not allow this. For the sake of his work and for the sake of his people, he will not let it continue. We must be awakened, whatever the cost. We cannot sleep like others. We must stay watchful and sober, for we are children of the light and of the day, not of the night or darkness. God will not permit us to waste life as though it were only for play. Duties done carelessly, prayers half-hearted, a life outwardly blameless but powerless and without influence, words wise perhaps but without depth or weight—these are things God will not tolerate in his saints. It is either the coldness of Sardis, to whom he says, “If you will not watch, I will come on you like a thief, and you will not know the hour I come upon you.” Or it is the lukewarmness of Laodicea, to whom he says, “Because you are lukewarm, neither cold nor hot, I will spit you out of my mouth.” In waking us, God begins gently. He touches us lightly, as the angel did Elijah under the tree, to stir us. He sends some small trial to shake us from our false rest. He lets us hear some distant sound—it may be the troubles of nations, or news of famine, war, or disease far away. If this fails, and we sleep on as before, he comes closer, and speaks through events nearer to home, perhaps among our own family. If this too fails, he comes still nearer, for time is short and his child is still asleep. He strikes at some tender spot until every nerve trembles and every heartbeat quickens. Our very soul is pierced through, as with many arrows. Then we start up like one waking from a long dream, and, looking around, wonder how we could have slept so long. But oh, how hard it is to truly wake us up! It takes blow after blow, over a long time, to do it. Even after we’ve been awakened, we constantly drift back to sleep. So we not only need to be awakened—we need to be kept awake. How much sorrow our spiritual sleepiness brings—how many broken, bleeding hearts! The comfort of “ease in Zion,” enjoyed perhaps for years, has come at a high cost. “Think of living,” said a thoughtful German. Another added, “Your life, even if you are the poorest soul on earth, is no idle dream, but a serious reality. It is your own. It’s all you have to face eternity with. So work like a star—unhurried, yet tireless.” Some Christians do work, but not like those who are truly awake. They carry out duties, but their steps are slow and heavy. Their efforts are forced and cold. They do good things, make good plans, say good words—but lack the vibrant energy of spiritual life. They have no burning zeal—no spring or strength in their labor. They don’t ignite others, and they themselves do not burn. The only thing star-like about them is their coldness. They are likely to face a sharp trial, because they need it. Others are only awake now and then. You can’t count on them, because their enthusiasm changes with their mood. Their naturally impulsive temper—which they may not even realize or try to discipline—makes them unstable. This kind of on-and-off wakefulness achieves little. They build up, then tear down. They ignite a flame, then put it out again. They too are not like stars. They need a deep, ongoing pressure to bring balance to their erratic spirit. Then there are those who always seem awake, but it’s just busy activity and restlessness. They can’t bear stillness. They must always be doing, planning, and moving. Their nervous, impatient nature makes silence and solitude feel like punishment. These people often don’t accomplish much themselves, but their constant activity stirs others and prevents stagnation. Still, their busyness keeps them from receiving the grace they need. Their endless involvement with outward religious things hinders their inward spiritual growth. They may seem like stars—restless and ever-moving—but they are losing the little light they had. They will likely need a hard, painful trial to strip away this false zeal and give them the steady spiritual wakefulness God calls them to. What they need is a deeper spiritual life, and that takes much refining. There are others who work steadily, and seem fervent too. But when you get to know them, you realize they’re not truly awake. They work far more than they pray, and so they soon become like lamps without oil. They may be further along than the previous group, but they still need awakening. They may resemble the star—calm and constant—but their light is dim. It shines faintly on the dark world. What they need is a deeper spiritual life and experience—and they too may face a hard trial to bring it about. The true awakened life is something else entirely. It is deep and intense. It has an atmosphere of calm strength and quiet dignity. It is passionate, but not frantic; active, but not rushed; wise, but not fearful or selfish; bold and firm, but not reckless. It doesn’t show off, and may even be silent—but those nearby can feel its influence. It is full of joy and peace, but without noise or display. It overflows with love and kindness, yet remains faithful and true. This is the wakeful life! But oh, how much we often have to suffer before we truly reach it. Our rebellious human nature resists full surrender to God. We struggle to become the living sacrifices He calls us to be—though it is our reasonable duty. In waking us from our spiritual sleep, God’s discipline doesn’t just make us more active or hardworking. It makes us far more prayerful. And perhaps this is where we feel the awakening most deeply. Nothing awakens prayer like trouble. It drives us straight to our knees, and closes the closet door behind us. In times of comfort, we have many distractions and sources of relief; in sorrow, we have only one—and that is God. Our grief is too deep to share with anyone else. No one else can carry it. Then we awaken to prayer. Before, it was something we did. Now, it becomes everything. Human help fails, and we are left leaning only on God. Our private prayer closets are truly the only places of light in a world that, to us, has grown doubly dark. Everything around us feels gloomy. The skies are clouded, and shadows cover all. But the closet is still bright—still peaceful. How eagerly, how gratefully we retreat there now. We could spend all our time in that quiet island of light that God has given us in the midst of life’s stormy sea. And when we must leave it for a time, how gladly we return. In that sacred solitude, with only God for company, we find hours of quiet peace. There, we can almost forget the storms still raging around us. Prayer becomes more real than ever. We now value it as we never did before. We cannot live without it. Out of both need and desire, we must pray—we must cry out from deep within. Our prayers are no longer formal or routine. Each one is now filled with real life, real energy, and deep earnestness. The heart prays now, and the lips can barely find words to express it. Often, it’s only the groanings that cannot be spoken which rise up to God. Before, we often had lips without the heart—now, more often, it is the heart without the lips. Now we begin to understand how “the Spirit helps our weaknesses.” We start to know what it means to “pray in the Holy Spirit.” There is a new closeness to God. Communion with Him becomes a clear, felt reality. It is deep, personal interaction with the living God. Fresh arguments rise in prayer, new desires are born, new needs come to light. Our emptiness and God’s rich fullness are shown to us more vividly than ever. The longings of our inmost soul are stirred, and our heart cries out for God—the living God. It was sorrow that awakened prayer in David. It was in the belly of the fish that Jonah learned to cry out to God. And it was in the thorny wilderness and the chains of Babylon that Manasseh learned to pray. Church of Christ—chosen people of the Lord—wake up! Children of the light, children of the day, rise! The long winter night is almost over. The morning star is about to rise. “The end of all things is at hand: therefore be sober, and watch unto prayer” (1 Peter 4:7). “Why are you sleeping? Rise and pray, so that you won’t fall into temptation!” (Luke 22:46).

item.title: The Night of Weeping: Help for the Suffering Family of God
item.dc:creator: horatiusbonar
item.content:encoded: Have you ever had a night of weeping? The road is rough, and the desert wind is harsh. For followers of the Man of Sorrows, trials are not exceptions, but inevitable companions on the journey toward the Kingdom. Whether you are walking in shadow or sunshine, in the crowd or in solitude, the Christian life is marked by the unique, tender, and often sorrowing voice of a pilgrim church. This small, intimate book is written specifically for the household of faith—especially those who are “in heaviness through many trials,” feeling the weight of the Lord’s rebuke, and passing through fire and water. Written by a fellow traveler—a brother and “companion in suffering”—this devotional seeks to move beyond mere sympathy and offer true, spiritual profit. It addresses the deepest family matters of the church: our shared sorrows and the meaning behind God’s correction.

item.title: Cross and Crown | Christian Martyrs & Church History
item.dc:creator: jamesdabneymccabe
item.content:encoded: Through fire, sword, and scaffold, the truth marched on across the centuries of church history. From the persecuted Christians of the early Church in the first centuries, to the Vaudois of the twelfth, the Huguenots of the sixteenth, and the English martyrs of the Reformation, this work revives the courage of men and women who stood unshaken for Christ. Their blood became the seed of freedom—the open Bible, the free press, and the liberties we now enjoy.

item.title: The Morning of Joy: Hope After the Night of Weeping
item.dc:creator: horatiusbonar
item.content:encoded: A companion to The Night of Weeping, this classic points weary saints toward the promised dawn. From the shadow of the cross, Horatius Bonar lifts our eyes to “the morning of joy,” tracing how God uses tears to ripen hope and how real comfort does more than soothe—it purifies. With rich, devotional chapters on anticipation, watchfulness, the “earnests” that break into our darkest nights, and the glory signaled by the Morning-Star, Bonar calls believers to live as children of the day: lamps trimmed, hands ready, hearts steady.

item.title: The King's Son: Billy Bray
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Billy Bray was born in a Cornish mining village in 1794, Billy Bray lived for years as a drunken, reckless miner—until one night in 1823 when the grace of God broke through his darkness and made him a new man. From that moment on, he could not stop talking about his Savior. His joy was contagious, his faith unshakable, and his voice could be heard ringing through the mines and the chapels of Cornwall: “I am a son of a King!”
The King’s Son tells the remarkable true story of how divine grace transformed a coarse, poverty-stricken miner into one of England’s most joyful and beloved evangelists. With humor, humility, and an unquenchable love for Christ, Billy Bray built chapels, lifted the downcast, and filled Cornwall with songs of praise. His story has inspired believers for generations to live and rejoice in the reality of redemption.

item.title: The Backslider: How Christians Drift—and Return
item.dc:creator: andrewfuller
item.content:encoded:
When faith grows dim and outward forms remain without life, what is left?
In The Backslider, Andrew Fuller exposes one of the most solemn truths in the Christian walk—the soul that once seemed earnest, yet turns away from God. With pastoral tenderness and searching insight into the human heart, Fuller traces the slow and hidden decline of the inner life: how holy affections fade, how conscience grows silent, and how sin—when left unconfessed—darkens the understanding and hardens the heart.
Yet this is not only a book of warning; it is a call to return. Fuller shows that restoration is found only through heartfelt repentance and renewed faith in Jesus Christ. For the wandering soul, the sorrowing shepherd, or the believer who trembles at the thought of falling away, The Backslider speaks with both conviction and mercy. A timeless appeal to self-examination—and to the grace that restores the soul to its first love.

item.title: Sending a Message with No Feet: The Cost of Trusting a Fool
item.dc:creator: charlesbridges
item.content:encoded:
Proverbs 26:6-9 — Sending a message by the hand of a fool is like cutting off one’s own feet or drinking violence. Like lame legs that hang limp is a proverb in the mouth of fools. Like binding a stone in a sling is giving honor to a fool. Like a thorn that sticks into the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of fools.

These pictures show just how dangerous and useless folly is, and they should stir us to pursue true wisdom. A fool is completely unfit for any important task. When we send a fool with a message, his errors—whether careless or deliberate—are so harmful it’s as if we’ve sent someone without feet. The result is injury and loss.

This was seen when the unbelieving spies spread doubt and rebellion among the people, causing great harm. It reminds us to place important responsibilities only in the hands of faithful, reliable people.

Fools either lack the ability to do their task or are driven by self-interest, even at the expense of those they serve. Solomon suffered harm by trusting an ambitious servant—who rebelled and took most of the kingdom from Solomon’s son. Ben-hadad was destroyed after sending a message through Hazael, who betrayed and murdered him to fulfill his own ambition.

See again how the fool exposes his shame. A lame man doesn’t show his weakness more than when he tries to display strength or agility. In the same way, a fool never looks more ridiculous than when he tries to appear wise. It’s off-putting.

A wise saying does not fit a fool—just like dancing doesn’t fit a cripple. A proverb—a serious, weighty saying—becomes a joke in his mouth. “Is Saul also among the prophets?” “Why do you look at the speck in your brother’s eye but ignore the beam in your own?” “Physician, heal yourself.” “You who teach others, do you not teach yourself?” These examples show how out of place wisdom sounds when spoken by the foolish.

Giving honor to a fool is just as dangerous. A stone tied into a sling becomes a deadly weapon. So does a fool given a place of honor—he becomes a danger to those around him.

One such fool, close to a powerful king, would have destroyed God’s chosen people—if God had not stepped in and stopped him. How dangerous it is to place unqualified people in power! It’s like handing a sword or a loaded gun to a madman.

The fool also harms himself—often without realizing it. Just as it is unfitting for a drunk man to grab a thorn bush, so it is for a fool to handle wise words. When a thorn pierces his hand, his numbness only makes the injury worse. In the same way, when a fool speaks a parable—a sharp, wise saying he barely understands—it pierces his own conscience. Yet he feels nothing.

What a sobering sight—the ungodly prophet speaking the very words of God with cold indifference, though those words are enough to make one tremble.

This is the fool: a danger to others, deeply accountable to God. And how much greater the danger and guilt when he takes on a sacred role! When the great message of God is delivered through ungodly messengers, the Church suffers serious harm.

The parable—the holy teaching of our Lord—when spoken by a fool, is twisted and contradicted by his life. “To the wicked God says: What right do you have to declare My statutes or take My covenant on your lips—since you hate instruction and cast My words behind you?”

O Almighty God, who alone does great wonders, send down upon all Bishops and ministers the life-giving power of Your grace. Raise up faithful stewards—workers unashamed—true ambassadors of Christ in Your Church. And may foolish, unfaithful ministers be corrected and restrained.

From Charles Bridges' expository commentary on Proverbs.

item.title: Exposition of Proverbs
item.dc:creator: charlesbridges
item.content:encoded: Charles Bridges’ Exposition of the Book of Proverbs is a timeless commentary that combines profound biblical insight with practical wisdom for everyday life. First published in the 19th century, Bridges’ work has guided generations of believers in understanding the moral and spiritual depth of Solomon’s proverbs. In this revised edition, the language has been carefully updated for readability while preserving Bridges’ original depth, warmth, and reverence for Scripture. The revision also incorporates quotations from the Berean Standard Bible (BSB), allowing readers to engage the text in clear, modern English without losing its biblical precision. Through Solomon’s inspired counsel, Bridges urges us to seek true wisdom, walk in the fear of the Lord, and avoid the snares of folly. His reflections illuminate how these ancient truths still speak with remarkable clarity and transforming power to the hearts of God’s people today.

item.title: Personal Prayer: How Prayer Works, with Testimonies of Answered Prayer
item.dc:creator: henryclaytrumbull
item.content:encoded: Listen to the free audiobook of Henry Clay Trumbull's book: Personal Prayer. With so many clear proofs of God’s guidance, whether in our own lives or in the lives of others, it is indeed strange that we are not quicker to pray and to trust—without doubting. Yet God does not grow weary with us. He never stops showing His loving patience. He remains faithful—not because we are so deserving, but because He is who He is, and He understands just how human we are. When a child of God goes through a personal experience—whether of joy or sorrow, struggle or encouragement—that seems unusual or unique, they often discover they are not alone. Many others have walked through similar paths, learning similar lessons under the same faithful hand of God. And whatever comfort or encouragement He gives to one of His children, He is willing to give to any who ask in faith, holding fast to His promises. That is why these testimonies of answered prayer have been gathered—not because they are the only ones the author has known, nor even the most remarkable, but because they are likely to help and strengthen ordinary Christians. The accounts in this book are drawn from the author’s own life and from the lives of those personally known to him. He has often shared such stories with younger believers—how he has brought specific needs to God, and how the Lord has clearly, tenderly answered. Again and again, those same believers return with joy, eager to tell how God has met them in just the same way, confirming that He truly hears and answers the prayers of His children. This book is offered to help you take God at His Word, and to encourage you to bring every need, great or small, to your Father in personal prayer.

item.title: How To Deal With Doubts and Doubters
item.dc:creator: henryclaytrumbull
item.content:encoded:
When doubt feels louder than truth, where do you turn? In How to Deal With Doubts and Doubters (1907, Revised 2025), Henry Clay Trumbull opens the door to real, heart-level conversations with troubled souls—men and women who feared they had gone too far, committed the “unpardonable sin,” lacked true faith, or could not feel what they thought a real Christian should feel. These are not theories, but actual encounters drawn from decades of ministry as a chaplain, pastor, and personal worker. Through vivid stories and clear, Christ-centered counsel, Trumbull shows:
  • How to help those who focus on what they don’t believe learn to rest in what they do believe
  • Why endlessly examining your feelings destroys assurance—and where to look instead
  • How to speak to people who think they must “feel something break inside” before they can come to Christ
  • How to comfort believers tormented by fear of the unpardonable sin
  • Why waiting to be “good enough,” or to “feel ready,” keeps many outside the church and away from obedience
  • How to think wisely about miracles, providence, and spiritual existence in a skeptical age
  • Why enjoying God’s service and prayer is not a spiritual danger, and when lack of enjoyment may simply be physical
Whether you are wrestling with doubts yourself or walking alongside someone who is, this classic 1907 work offers warm, practical help. Trumbull refuses shallow answers. Instead, he patiently leads doubting, sensitive souls away from themselves and back to the living Christ—where lasting rest is found. Ideal for pastors, counselors, small group leaders, and any Christian who wants to deal gently and wisely with wounded consciences.
